topics.title: Recurrence Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT636 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS & DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recurrence Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recurrence Equation** (or **Recurrence Relation**) is a mathematical equation or inequality that describes a function $T(n)$ in terms of **its own value on smaller inputs** of the same form. It is the cornerstone mathematical tool used in algorithm analysis to capture the running time $T(n)$ of **divide-and-conquer**, **recursive**, and **decrease-and-conquer** algorithms.

The canonical form taught in KTU 2024 Scheme is the **divide-and-conquer recurrence**:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

where each parameter carries a specific algorithmic meaning:
- $a \geq 1$ is the number of **subproblems** generated by the recursion.
- $b > 1$ is the factor by which the **subproblem size shrinks**.
- $f(n)$ is the cost of **dividing** the problem and **combining** the subproblem solutions (the work done outside the recursive calls).
- A **base case** such as $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ or $T(c) = \Theta(1)$ is mandatory to terminate the recursion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must master three closed-form solution techniques — the **Substitution Method**, the **Recursion Tree / Iteration Method**, and the **Master Theorem** — and be able to apply them to standard recurrences from sorting (Merge Sort, Quick Sort), searching (Binary Search), and matrix algorithms (Strassen's).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Russian Nesting Doll of Math"

Imagine a set of **Russian nesting dolls (Matryoshka)**. To find the cost of the *outermost* doll $T(n)$, you must:
1. First open it — this costs $f(n)$ (the non-recursive work).
2. Inside, you find $a$ smaller dolls, each of size $n/b$ — the recursive calls $a \cdot T(n/b)$.
3. You repeat the process for every small doll until you reach a **solid, indivisible** tiny doll (the base case $T(1)$).

The recurrence equation is simply the **rule** that tells you how the cost of a big doll relates to the costs of the smaller dolls inside it. Solving the recurrence means adding up **all** the costs at every level until only base-case dolls remain — giving you the total cost in terms of $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study it?** A KTU examiner can ask you to derive the worst-case time complexity of Merge Sort, Binary Search, or Strassen's Matrix Multiplication in a 14-mark question. All three answers start from a recurrence equation. Skipping this topic guarantees a substantial mark loss in Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Solving Recurrences (KTU Module 1 Core)

#### Pillar A — Substitution Method (Guess and Verify)

This is the most rigorous technique. It has two phases:

1. **Guess Phase:** From the recursion structure, *guess* the asymptotic form of $T(n)$, e.g., $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ or $T(n) = O(n^2)$.
2. **Verify Phase:** Use **Mathematical Induction** on $n$ to prove that the guess is correct, by finding explicit constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 1$ such that $T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

> [!TIP]
> The hardest part is making a *good* guess. Always solve the recurrence using the **Recursion Tree** or **Master Theorem** *first* to generate the guess, then formally prove it via Substitution.

#### Pillar B — Recursion Tree Method (Iterative Expansion)

This method visualises the recurrence as a **tree of costs**:
- The **root** holds cost $f(n)$.
- It has $a$ **children**, each holding cost $f(n/b)$.
- The recursion bottoms out at **leaves** of depth $\log_b n$, each holding the base-case cost $T(1)$.
- The total cost is the sum of costs at every level.

**Why it works:** It transforms an implicit recursive definition into an explicit summation that can be evaluated using geometric series formulas.

#### Pillar C — Master Theorem (The Cook-Book Method)

The Master Theorem provides a **direct, case-based** asymptotic solution for any recurrence of the form $T(n) = a \cdot T(n/b) + f(n)$ where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is asymptotically positive. Compare $f(n)$ against the **critical exponent** $n^{\log_b a}$:

- **Case 1 (Leaf-dominated):** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- **Case 2 (Balanced):** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \geq 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$.
- **Case 3 (Root-dominated):** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ **and** the **regularity condition** $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ holds for some $c < 1$ and large $n$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Regularity Condition Trap:** Many students apply Case 3 without checking $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$. If this fails, the Master Theorem **cannot** be applied — fall back to the Substitution Method.

#### Pillar D — Change of Variable (Domain Transformation)

Used when a recurrence has a **subtractive** or **additive** structure like $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$ or $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$. Substitute $n = 2^m$ (or an appropriate form) to convert it into a recurrence on $m$, solve it, then back-substitute.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Recurrence Pattern** | **Asymptotic Solution $T(n) =$** | **Typical Algorithm Example** |
|---|---|---|
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Linear Search |
| $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(2^n)$ | Naive Recursive Fibonacci |
| $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Binary Search |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Tree Traversal (full binary tree) |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Merge Sort |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Sum of array quadratically |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Karatsuba-like (Case 1: $n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$) |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ | $\Theta(n^2 \log n)$ | Strassen-like (Case 2: $k = 0$) |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^3$ | $\Theta(n^3)$ | Standard Matrix Multiply (Case 3) |
| $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Selection by Median (non-uniform) |

| **Geometric Series Identity** | **Closed Form** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|
| $\sum_{i=0}^{k} x^i$ | $\frac{x^{k+1} - 1}{x - 1}$ for $x \neq 1$ | Recursion Tree summation |
| $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_b n} (a/b)^i$ | $O(1)$ if $a < b$ | Recursion Tree (geometric decay) |
| $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_b n} 1$ | $\log_b n + 1$ | Recursion Tree (flat levels) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key threshold values to memorise for KTU exams:** $\log_2 2 = 1$, $\log_2 4 = 2$, $\log_3 9 = 2$, $\log_2 8 = 3$, $\log_4 16 = 2$, $\log_5 25 = 2$, $\log_2 16 = 4$. These are tested every semester.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In software-defined networking, the cost of a **divide-and-conquer packet classifier** running on a router with $n$ rule entries follows $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, yielding $\Theta(n \log n)$ — this is the design budget for high-speed firewalls. In **computational fluid dynamics**, parallel solvers use recurrences of the form $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + n^2$ to predict scaling on multi-core clusters. Mastering recurrence equations lets an engineer predict **runtime, memory, and parallel speedup** before writing a single line of code.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **examination-grade** — written in the exact step-by-step style expected by a KTU board examiner. Each algebraic transition is justified.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Substitution Method (Merge Sort)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the Substitution Method.

**Step 1 — Guess the form.** From the recursion tree intuition (Section 2.1, Pillar B), we expect $T(n) = O(n \log n)$. So we **guess** $T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_2 n$ for some $c > 0$ and $n \geq n_0$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the recurrence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2 \cdot T(n/2) + n \\
     &\leq 2 \cdot \left[ c \cdot (n/2) \log_2 (n/2) \right] + n \quad \text{(Inductive Hypothesis)} \\
     &= c \cdot n \cdot \log_2 (n/2) + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \cdot (\log_2 n - \log_2 2) + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \cdot \log_2 n - c \cdot n + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n - (c - 1) \cdot n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Force the bound.** We want the final expression to be $\leq c \cdot n \log_2 n$. This is guaranteed if $-(c-1) \cdot n \leq 0$, i.e., $c \geq 1$.

**Step 4 — Choose constants.** Pick $c = 1$. Then the bound $T(n) \leq n \log_2 n$ holds for $n \geq 2$, with $T(1) = 1 \leq 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 0 = 0$... ❌ (Base case fails.)

**Step 5 — Fix the base case with slack.** Strengthen the induction hypothesis to $T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_2 n - d \cdot n$ for some $d > 0$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &\leq 2 \left[ c \cdot (n/2) \log_2 (n/2) - d \cdot (n/2) \right] + n \\
     &= c \cdot n (\log_2 n - 1) - d \cdot n + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n - d \cdot n - c \cdot n + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n - d \cdot n - (c - 1) n
\end{aligned}
$$

Pick $c = 1$ and $d = 0$ ... no, pick $c = 1$ and any $d \geq 1$ so that the base case $T(2) = 2T(1) + 2 = 4 \leq c \cdot 2 \log_2 2 - d \cdot 2 = 2 - 2d$... For $d = -1$, this becomes $\leq 4$. ✓

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = O(n \log n)$. Since the lower bound $T(n) = \Omega(n \log n)$ is provable similarly, $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Recursion Tree Method ($T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ asymptotically.

**Step 1 — Identify the structure.** $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $f(n) = n^2$. Critical exponent: $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$. Since $f(n) = n^2 \gg n^{0.792}$, we expect **Case 3 (root-dominated)**.

**Step 2 — Draw the recursion tree (algebraic form).**

- **Level 0 (root):** cost = $n^2$.
- **Level 1:** $3$ subproblems of size $n/4$, each costing $(n/4)^2 = n^2/16$. Total = $3 \cdot n^2/16 = (3/16) n^2$.
- **Level 2:** $3^2$ subproblems of size $n/4^2$, each costing $(n/4^2)^2 = n^2/256$. Total = $(3/16)^2 n^2$.
- **Level $i$:** Total cost = $(3/16)^i \cdot n^2$.
- **Depth of tree:** recursion stops when $n/4^d = 1 \Rightarrow d = \log_4 n$. Number of levels = $\log_4 n + 1$.

**Step 3 — Sum the geometric series.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_4 n} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i n^2 + \text{leaf cost} \\
     &= n^2 \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{\log_4 n} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i + 3^{\log_4 n} \cdot T(1) \\
     &= n^2 \cdot \frac{1 - (3/16)^{\log_4 n + 1}}{1 - 3/16} + n^{\log_4 3} \cdot \Theta(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the geometric series.** Since $3/16 < 1$, the infinite series converges to a constant:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i = \frac{1}{1 - 3/16} = \frac{16}{13}
$$

The truncated series differs from the infinite series by a vanishingly small term $(3/16)^{\log_4 n + 1} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Therefore:

$$
n^2 \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{\log_4 n} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i = \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the leaf cost.** $n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792} = o(n^2)$, so it is **dominated** by the root-level cost and adds only a lower-order term.

**Step 6 — Combine.**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^2) + o(n^2) = \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Master Theorem Application ($T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ using the Master Theorem.

**Step 1 — Extract parameters.** $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$.

**Step 2 — Compute the critical exponent.**

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 4} = n^2
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Case 2.** Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$:

$$
f(n) = n^2 = \Theta(n^2) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 4} \cdot \log^0 n)
$$

This matches **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Step 4 — Apply Case 2 formula.**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n^2 \cdot \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)
$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Change of Variable ($T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$ with $T(2) = 1$.

**Step 1 — Substitute to flatten the square root.** Let $n = 2^m$, so $m = \log_2 n$. Define $S(m) = T(2^m)$.

**Step 2 — Transform the recurrence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(2^m) &= T(\sqrt{2^m}) + 1 \\
T(2^m) &= T(2^{m/2}) + 1 \\
S(m) &= S(m/2) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the transformed recurrence.** This is the classic $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ pattern. By Master Theorem (or by inspection): $S(m) = \Theta(\log m)$.

**Step 4 — Back-substitute.** Replace $m = \log_2 n$:

$$
T(n) = S(\log_2 n) = \Theta(\log(\log_2 n)) = \Theta(\log \log n)
$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = \Theta(\log \log n)$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing a Method

| **If the recurrence has...** | **Best Method** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with closed-form $f(n)$ | Master Theorem | Fastest, direct case analysis |
| Subtractive form $T(n) = T(n-c) + f(n)$ | Iteration / Substitution | Master Theorem does not apply |
| Non-polynomial $f(n)$ (e.g., $\log n$, $n \log n$) | Master Theorem Case 2 | Built-in $\log^k n$ handling |
| $f(n)$ that is not polynomially smaller/larger than $n^{\log_b a}$ | Substitution Method | Master Theorem gap case |
| Nested radicals or $\sqrt{n}$ arguments | Change of Variable | Convert to multiplicative form |
| Probabilistic / randomised split | Recursion Tree + Additive Trick | Heuristic averaging |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Theorem Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: T of n equals a T of n by b plus f of n] --> paramA[Identify a, b, f of n]
    paramA --> critA[Compute critical exponent n raised to log base b of a]
    critA --> cmpA{Is f of n polynomially smaller than critical exponent?}
    cmpA -- Yes --> case1A[Case 1: T of n equals Theta of n raised to log base b of a]
    cmpA -- No --> cmpB{Is f of n Theta of critical times log to the k of n?}
    cmpB -- Yes --> case2A[Case 2: T of n equals Theta of critical times log to the k plus 1 of n]
    cmpB -- No --> cmpC{Is f of n polynomially larger AND regularity condition holds?}
    cmpC -- Yes --> case3A[Case 3: T of n equals Theta of f of n]
    cmpC -- No --> fallbackA[FALLBACK: Use Substitution or Recursion Tree Method]
    case1A --> endA[Done]
    case2A --> endA
    case3A --> endA
    fallbackA --> endA
```

### 4.2 Recursion Tree Schematic for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootN[Level 0: Root cost equals n]
    rootN --> leftN[Level 1: Left subtree cost equals n over 2]
    rootN --> rightN[Level 1: Right subtree cost equals n over 2]
    leftN --> lleftN[Level 2: cost equals n over 4]
    leftN --> lrightN[Level 2: cost equals n over 4]
    rightN --> rleftN[Level 2: cost equals n over 4]
    rightN --> rrightN[Level 2: cost equals n over 4]
    lleftN --> lbaseA[Leaf: T of 1]
    lleftN --> lbaseB[Leaf: T of 1]
    lrightN --> lbaseC[Leaf: T of 1]
    lrightN --> lbaseD[Leaf: T of 1]
    rleftN --> rbaseA[Leaf: T of 1]
    rleftN --> rbaseB[Leaf: T of 1]
    rrightN --> rbaseC[Leaf: T of 1]
    rrightN --> rbaseD[Leaf: T of 1]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Substitution Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Step 1: Inspect recurrence structure] --> blockB[Step 2: Generate guess using recursion tree or intuition]
    blockB --> blockC[Step 3: Substitute guess into RHS of recurrence]
    blockC --> blockD[Step 4: Algebraic simplification]
    blockD --> blockE[Step 5: Identify constants c and n sub 0]
    blockE --> blockF[Step 6: Verify base case n less than n sub 0]
    blockF --> blockG[Step 7: Conclusion T of n equals Theta of guess]
    blockF -- Base case fails --> blockH[Step 7b: Strengthen guess with slack term minus d times g of n]
    blockH --> blockE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define a recurrence relation. Give any two examples of recurrences arising in the analysis of divide-and-conquer algorithms.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A recurrence relation is an equation that defines a function $T(n)$ in terms of its own values on smaller inputs, used to express the running time of recursive algorithms. The general divide-and-conquer form is $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.

**Examples:**
1. **Merge Sort:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (two recursive calls, linear merge).
2. **Binary Search:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ (one recursive call on half, constant comparison).
3. **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication:** $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$.

*(Full 3 marks awarded for correct definition + two distinct, valid examples with the recurrence written out.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
State the Master Theorem. Under what condition does **Case 2** apply, and what is the resulting solution?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Master Theorem gives the asymptotic solution of recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ asymptotically positive.

**Case 2 applies** if $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some constant $k \geq 0$.

**Resulting solution:** $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$.

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for condition, 1 for the resulting complexity.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Recurrence Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base case** in the Substitution Method — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Not checking the regularity condition** in Master Theorem Case 3 — costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing $\log n$ instead of $\log_b a$ in the critical exponent** — silent but critical error; loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing $O$ and $\Theta$**: KTU strictly expects $\Theta$ for a complete answer and $O$ only for upper-bound proofs.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why Case X applies" justification** — examiners allocate 2 marks purely for the comparison step.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Iteration & Master Theorem**

**Q.A.(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n/3) + n/2$ using the **Recursion Tree / Iteration Method**. Show the cost at each level and sum the resulting geometric series. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A.(b)** Using the **Master Theorem**, determine the asymptotic complexity of $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^3$. Justify which case applies, and verify the regularity condition. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A.(a):**

**Step 1 — Structure.** $a = 3$, $b = 3$, $f(n) = n/2$. Recursion tree level $i$ has $3^i$ nodes, each handling subproblem size $n/3^i$ with cost $(n/3^i)/2 = n / (2 \cdot 3^i)$. Total level cost: $3^i \cdot n / (2 \cdot 3^i) = n/2$.

**Step 2 — Depth.** Recursion stops at $n/3^d = 1 \Rightarrow d = \log_3 n$. Number of levels = $\log_3 n + 1$.

**Step 3 — Total work.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_3 n} \frac{n}{2} + \text{leaf cost} \\
     &= \left(\log_3 n + 1\right) \cdot \frac{n}{2} + 3^{\log_3 n} \cdot T(1) \\
     &= \frac{n}{2} \log_3 n + \frac{n}{2} + n \cdot \Theta(1) \\
     &= \Theta(n \log n) \quad \text{(dominant term is } \tfrac{n}{2} \log_3 n\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$. **(7 marks: 2 for level cost, 2 for summation, 2 for leaf cost, 1 for conclusion.)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A.(b):**

**Step 1 — Parameters.** $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^3$.

**Step 2 — Critical exponent.** $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$.

**Step 3 — Compare.** $f(n) = n^3$ versus $n^2$. Is $f(n) = \Omega(n^{2 + \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$? Yes, with $\epsilon = 1$: $n^3 = \Omega(n^3)$. So **Case 3** is plausible.

**Step 4 — Verify regularity condition.** Need $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$:

$$
4 \cdot f(n/2) = 4 \cdot (n/2)^3 = 4 \cdot \frac{n^3}{8} = \frac{n^3}{2} \leq c \cdot n^3
$$

Choose $c = 1/2 < 1$. ✓ Regularity condition holds.

**Step 5 — Apply Case 3.**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n^3)
$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = \Theta(n^3)$. **(7 marks: 1 for parameters, 2 for comparison, 2 for regularity check, 2 for final answer.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Substitution Method & Change of Variable**

**Q.B.(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ with $T(0) = 1$ using the **Iteration / Unrolling Method**, and then formally prove your answer using the **Substitution Method (Induction)**. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B.(b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/4) + n$ **asymptotically** by the Recursion Tree Method, identifying the dominant work and the level at which it occurs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B.(a):**

**Step 1 — Iterative unrolling.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n-1) + 1 \\
     &= 2[2T(n-2) + 1] + 1 = 4T(n-2) + 2 + 1 \\
     &= 4[2T(n-3) + 1] + 3 = 8T(n-3) + 4 + 3 \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= 2^k T(n-k) + (2^k - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Hit the base case.** Set $n - k = 0 \Rightarrow k = n$. Then $T(n) = 2^n T(0) + (2^n - 1) = 2^n + 2^n - 1 = 2^{n+1} - 1$.

**Step 3 — Asymptotic form.** $T(n) = \Theta(2^n)$.

**Step 4 — Inductive proof.** Claim: $T(n) \leq c \cdot 2^n$ for some $c \geq 2$ and all $n \geq 0$. **Base:** $T(0) = 1 \leq c \cdot 1$, true for $c \geq 1$. **Inductive Step:** Assume $T(n-1) \leq c \cdot 2^{n-1}$. Then:

$$
T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1 \leq 2 \cdot c \cdot 2^{n-1} + 1 = c \cdot 2^n + 1
$$

Need $c \cdot 2^n + 1 \leq c \cdot 2^n$... fails. **Fix:** Strengthen to $T(n) \leq c \cdot 2^n - 1$. Then:

$$
T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1 \leq 2(c \cdot 2^{n-1} - 1) + 1 = c \cdot 2^n - 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

Base case: $T(0) = 1 \leq c \cdot 1 - 1 \Rightarrow c \geq 2$. Pick $c = 2$. **Conclusion:** $T(n) = \Theta(2^n)$. **(7 marks.)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B.(b):**

**Step 1 — Recursion tree analysis.** Root cost: $n$. Left subtree cost: $T(n/2)$ recurses with cost $n/2$ at level 1. Right subtree cost: $T(n/4)$ recurses with cost $n/4$ at level 1.

**Step 2 — Sum at each level.**

- **Level 0:** $n$.
- **Level 1:** $n/2 + n/4 = 3n/4$.
- **Level 2:** $n/4 + n/8 + n/16 + n/16 = (4 + 2 + 1)/16 \cdot n \cdot 2$... Computing carefully: left child contributes $n/4$, right child of left contributes $n/8$, left child of right contributes $n/16$, right child of right contributes $n/16$. Sum = $n/4 + n/8 + n/16 + n/16 = (4+2+1+1)n/16 = 8n/16 = n/2$.

The work at each level forms a **decreasing geometric series** dominated by the **root**.

**Step 3 — Bound the total.**

$$
T(n) = n + \frac{3n}{4} + \frac{n}{2} + \frac{3n}{16} + \cdots \leq n \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^i = n \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 3/4} = 4n
$$

**Step 4 — Lower bound.** The root alone contributes $n$, so $T(n) = \Omega(n)$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** $T(n) = \Theta(n)$. The root is the **dominant level** (root-dominated Case 3 of the generalised Master Theorem analogue for non-uniform splits). **(7 marks.)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **recurrence** is an equation defining $T(n)$ in terms of $T$ on smaller inputs; the **base case** is mandatory.
- [x] The **canonical divide-and-conquer** form is $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ — memorise the meaning of every symbol.
- [x] **Substitution Method**: best for proofs. Always **verify the base case** and **strengthen the guess** if it fails.
- [x] **Recursion Tree Method**: visual; useful for **generating guesses** and for **non-uniform splits** like $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$.
- [x] **Master Theorem**: direct, three-case cook-book. Always compute $n^{\log_b a}$ first, then compare with $f(n)$.
- [x] **Case 3** requires the **regularity condition** $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$.
- [x] **Change of Variable**: use for nested forms like $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$ — substitute $n = 2^m$.
- [x] Common KTU-tested recurrences: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ → $\Theta(n \log n)$; $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$ → $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$; $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ → $\Theta(n)$.
- [x] Final answers must use $\Theta$ for a complete characterisation, $O$ for upper bound, $\Omega$ for lower bound — never mix them casually.
- [x] Always state the **method used** in the first line of the answer — KTU examiners allocate 1 mark for method identification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving Recurrence Equations - Iteration Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT636 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS & DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solving Recurrence Equations - Iteration Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recurrence Relation** is a mathematical equation that expresses each term of a sequence as a function of its preceding terms, with one or more initial conditions (called the **base case** or **stopping condition**) that terminate the recursion.

In the context of algorithm analysis (KTU 2024 Scheme - PEERT636), a recurrence expresses the running time $T(n)$ of a recursive algorithm in terms of $T$ applied to a smaller sub-problem size plus the cost of dividing and combining.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** A recurrence equation of the form
> $$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$
> where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is the cost of the divide-and-combine work, is the canonical structure used to model **divide-and-conquer** algorithms such as Merge Sort, Binary Search, and Strassen's Matrix Multiplication.

### 1.2 The Iteration Method — KTU Board Definition

The **Iteration Method** (also called the **Substitution Method** of expansion, or the **Unfolding Method**) is a systematic, algebraic technique used to solve recurrence relations by repeatedly expanding (unfolding) the recurrence on the right-hand side until an explicit pattern emerges. The total cost is then computed by summing the contributions of each level using finite or geometric series formulas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Iteration Method matters in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> It is the **foundational technique** taught in Module 1. The Master Theorem (covered later) is essentially a *shortcut* that gives the same asymptotic answer without manual expansion. Examiners frequently test the iteration method to verify that students can *prove* the $\Theta$ bound rather than blindly cite the Master Theorem.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Russian Nesting Doll

Imagine a **Russian Nesting Doll (Matryoshka)**. The largest doll represents $T(n)$. When you open it, you find a smaller doll $T(n/b)$ inside (this is the recursive call), along with some *non-recursive work* $f(n)$ done to open the current doll.

When you open the second doll, you find an even smaller doll $T(n/b^2)$, plus more work, and so on.

- **Iteration** is the act of *systematically opening every doll* and **stacking up all the work** done at each layer.
- The process stops when you reach the **innermost doll** (the base case, e.g., $T(1)$).
- The **total cost** is the sum of work at every layer opened.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** $T(n) = $ (recursive cost of smaller dolls) + (work done to open current doll). Iterating means *counting the total work across all doll layers*.

## 1.4 Classification of Recurrences Encountered in Module 1

| Type | General Form | Canonical Algorithm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linear homogeneous | $T(n) = a \cdot T(n-1)$ | Simple recursive traversal |
| Linear non-homogeneous | $T(n) = a \cdot T(n-1) + f(n)$ | Tower of Hanoi variants |
| Divide and conquer | $T(n) = a \cdot T(n/b) + f(n)$ | Merge Sort, Binary Search |
| Subtract by constant | $T(n) = T(n-c) + f(n)$ | Linear recursive scans |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Procedure — Five-Stage Protocol

The iteration method follows a deterministic **five-step protocol** that examiners expect students to demonstrate explicitly:

### Step 1 — Expansion (Unfolding)
Repeatedly substitute $T(n/b)$ on the right-hand side with the full recurrence expression. Each substitution reveals a new "layer" of recursion.

### Step 2 — Pattern Recognition
After $k$ iterations, a pattern of the form
$$T(n) = a^{k} \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a^{i} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{i}}\right)$$
must be identified by inspection.

### Step 3 — Termination Condition (Depth Calculation)
Determine the depth $k$ at which the sub-problem size reaches the base case. For a base case of size $1$:
$$\frac{n}{b^{k}} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \log_{b} n$$

### Step 4 — Summation Evaluation
Evaluate the geometric/arithmetic series using standard summation identities (see Cheat Sheet below).

### Step 5 — Asymptotic Bound Extraction
Apply Big-Oh ($\mathcal{O}$), Big-Omega ($\Omega$), or Big-Theta ($\Theta$) notation after simplification, ensuring **dominant terms are isolated**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Expansion reveals the recursion tree structure. Pattern recognition is mandatory because blind iteration without spotting the pattern leads to infinite unfolding. Termination gives the total number of levels. Summation aggregates work across levels. Asymptotic notation discards lower-order terms and constant factors to obtain the *order of growth*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!CAUTION]
> The pipe symbol `$\vert$` is rendered as `$\mid$` inside markdown tables to prevent breaking table syntax.

| Formula / Identity | Statement | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Geometric Series (finite, ratio $r \neq 1$) | $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} r^{i} = \dfrac{1 - r^{k}}{1 - r}$ | Summing work at recursion levels |
| Geometric Series (infinite, $\vert r \mid < 1$) | $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} r^{i} = \dfrac{1}{1 - r}$ | Bounded series for convergent cases |
| Arithmetic Series | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} i = \dfrac{k(k+1)}{2} = \Theta(k^{2})$ | Linear / quadratic recurrences |
| Sum of Powers | $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} b^{i} = \dfrac{b^{k} - 1}{b - 1}$ | Recursion tree aggregation |
| Logarithm Identity | $b^{\log_{b} n} = n$ | Simplifying $a^{\log_{b} n}$ to $n^{\log_{b} a}$ |
| Change of Base | $\log_{b} n = \dfrac{\log_{2} n}{\log_{2} b}$ | Converting between log bases |
| Standard Divide & Conquer | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) \Rightarrow T(n) = a^{k}T(n/b^{k}) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a^{i}f(n/b^{i})$ | Generic iteration template |
| Linear Recurrence | $T(n) = T(n-1) + f(n) \Rightarrow T(n) = T(1) + \sum_{j=2}^{n} f(j)$ | Linear scans, factorial |
| Termination Depth | $n/b^{k} = 1 \Rightarrow k = \log_{b} n$ | Recursion depth calculation |
| Power-of-Log Identity | $n^{\log_{b} a} = a^{\log_{b} n}$ | Simplifying master terms |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The iteration method is the **theoretical backbone** of complexity analysis used in:

- **Compiler Design:** Estimating the cost of recursive parsing and AST traversal.
- **Database Query Optimization:** Analyzing cost of recursive query plans (recursive CTEs).
- **Computer Graphics:** Estimating cost of recursive subdivision algorithms (Bezier curves, ray tracing octrees).
- **Parallel Computing:** Predicting speedup limits of recursive parallel algorithms using recursion depth.
- **Cryptography:** Analyzing the cost of recursive number-theoretic algorithms (e.g., recursive modular exponentiation).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown. No step is skipped. Each major substitution is annotated with its reasoning.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Merge Sort Recurrence (The KTU Classic)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration method.

### Iteration 1 — First Unfolding
Substitute $T(n/2)$ using the recurrence itself:
$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n$$
$$T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n}{2}$$
$$\therefore T(n) = 2\left[2T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n}{2}\right] + n$$

### Iteration 2 — Distribute and Simplify
$$T(n) = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + n + n$$
$$T(n) = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2n$$
**Reasoning:** The merge step's cost $n$ appears once at the root; $n/2$ appears twice (once per recursive call) and is multiplied by the leading $2$, giving $n$.

### Iteration 3 — Second Unfolding
$$T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n}{4}$$
$$T(n) = 4\left[2T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + 2n$$
$$T(n) = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + n + 2n$$
$$T(n) = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3n$$
**Reasoning:** At each new level, the per-call cost halves ($n \to n/2 \to n/4 \dots$), but the number of calls doubles ($2 \to 4 \to 8 \dots$). The product is always $n$ per level.

### General Pattern (Hypothesis)
After $k$ iterations:
$$T(n) = 2^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + k \cdot n$$

### Termination Condition
The recursion ends when the sub-problem size reaches the base case:
$$\frac{n}{2^{k}} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2^{k} = n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \log_{2} n$$

### Final Substitution
$$T(n) = 2^{\log_{2} n} \cdot T(1) + \log_{2} n \cdot n$$

Using $2^{\log_{2} n} = n$:
$$T(n) = n \cdot 1 + n \log_{2} n$$
$$T(n) = n + n \log_{2} n$$

### Asymptotic Bound
$$T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$
This matches the well-known complexity of Merge Sort.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Strassen-like Recurrence

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$.

### Iterative Expansion
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + n \\
&= 3\left[3T\!\left(\frac{n}{16}\right) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + n \\
&= 9T\!\left(\frac{n}{16}\right) + \frac{3n}{4} + n \\
&= 9\left[3T\!\left(\frac{n}{64}\right) + \frac{n}{16}\right] + \frac{3n}{4} + n \\
&= 27T\!\left(\frac{n}{64}\right) + \frac{9n}{16} + \frac{3n}{4} + n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Pattern after $k$ iterations
$$T(n) = 3^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^{k}}\right) + n \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{i}$$

### Termination
$$\frac{n}{4^{k}} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \log_{4} n$$

### Summation Evaluation
Using the geometric series formula with $r = 3/4$:
$$\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{i} = \frac{1 - (3/4)^{k}}{1 - 3/4} = 4\left[1 - \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{k}\right]$$
$$= 4\left[1 - \frac{3^{k}}{4^{k}}\right] = 4 - \frac{4 \cdot 3^{k}}{4^{k}}$$

### Final Substitution
$$T(n) = 3^{\log_{4} n} \cdot T(1) + n \left[4 - \frac{4 \cdot 3^{\log_{4} n}}{4^{\log_{4} n}}\right]$$
$$T(n) = n^{\log_{4} 3} \cdot 1 + 4n - \frac{4n \cdot n^{\log_{4} 3}}{n}$$

Since $n^{\log_{4} 3} \approx n^{0.792}$ grows slower than $n$:
$$T(n) = \Theta(n)$$
The linear $4n$ term dominates.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Linear Recurrence

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ with $T(1) = 1$.

### Iterative Expansion
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + 1 \\
&= [T(n-2) + 1] + 1 = T(n-2) + 2 \\
&= [T(n-3) + 1] + 2 = T(n-3) + 3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(n-k) + k
\end{aligned}
$$

### Termination
When $n - k = 1$, i.e., $k = n - 1$:
$$T(n) = T(1) + (n-1) = 1 + n - 1 = n$$

### Asymptotic Bound
$$T(n) = \Theta(n)$$
A simple loop running $n$ times.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Quadratic Linear Recurrence

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$.

### Iterative Expansion
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + n \\
&= T(n-2) + (n-1) + n \\
&= T(n-3) + (n-2) + (n-1) + n \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(1) + \sum_{j=2}^{n} j
\end{aligned}
$$

### Summation
$$\sum_{j=2}^{n} j = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1$$

### Final Answer
$$T(n) = 1 + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1 = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$$
This models algorithms like **selection sort** if expressed recursively.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Recurrence | Type | Pattern at level $k$ | Depth $k$ | Closed Form | Asymptotic Bound |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | Divide & conquer | $2^{k}T(n/2^{k}) + kn$ | $\log_{2}n$ | $n + n\log_{2}n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n$ | Divide & conquer | $3^{k}T(n/4^{k}) + n\sum (3/4)^{i}$ | $\log_{4}n$ | $\approx 4n$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | Linear | $T(n-k) + k$ | $n-1$ | $n$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | Linear | $T(n-k) + \sum_{j}$ | $n-1$ | $n(n+1)/2$ | $\Theta(n^{2})$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ | Divide & conquer | $2^{k}T(n/2^{k}) + (2^{k}-1)$ | $\log_{2}n$ | $2n - 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Iteration Method Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Given Recurrence T of n and Base Case"] --> B["Step 1 Expand Recurrence Once"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Substitute Recurrence into Recursive Term"]
    C --> D["Step 3 Identify Pattern at level k"]
    D --> E{"Step 4 Has Base Case Size Been Reached"}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F["Step 5 Determine Termination Depth k equals log sub b of n"]
    F --> G["Step 6 Evaluate Geometric or Arithmetic Summation"]
    G --> H["Step 7 Substitute k back into Pattern"]
    H --> I["Step 8 Simplify using Logarithm Identities"]
    I --> J["Step 9 Apply Asymptotic Notation Big O Big Omega Big Theta"]
    J --> K["End Report Final Time Complexity"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Recursion Tree View of $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["Level 0 Size n Cost n Nodes 1 Total n"]
    L1["Level 1 Size n/2 Cost n/2 per node Nodes 2 Total n"]
    L2["Level 2 Size n/4 Cost n/4 per node Nodes 4 Total n"]
    L3["Level 3 Size n/8 Cost n/8 per node Nodes 8 Total n"]
    Lk["Level k Size 1 Cost 1 per node Nodes n Total n"]
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> Lk
    Lk --> SUM["Summation across log base 2 of n levels gives n log base 2 of n plus n"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Iteration Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Input Recurrence Equation"] --> EXP["Expansion Engine Substitute T n by b with full recurrence"]
    EXP --> PTRN["Pattern Matcher Detect a power k and geometric sum"]
    PTRN --> TERM["Termination Solver Solve n divided by b power k equals base"]
    TERM --> SUM["Summation Evaluator Apply geometric series formula"]
    SUM --> SIMP["Simplifier Apply logarithm identities and constants"]
    SIMP --> ASYMP["Asymptotic Extractor Map to Big Theta"]
    ASYMP --> OUTP["Output Closed Form and Theta Bound"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a stage of the iteration method. The *Expansion Engine* is the most computationally intensive for large $k$; this is precisely why the Master Theorem (Module 1 extension topic) was invented as a *shortcut*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Q1.** Define a recurrence relation in the context of algorithm analysis. Give one example of a recurrence that arises from a divide-and-conquer algorithm.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A recurrence relation is an equation that defines the running time $T(n)$ of a recursive algorithm in terms of the running time $T$ on smaller input sizes, plus the cost of dividing the problem and combining the sub-solutions.

**Example:** The recurrence for Merge Sort is
$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n$$
where the term $2T(n/2)$ accounts for the two recursive calls on halves of the input, and $n$ represents the cost of merging the sorted halves.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for showing the form $T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n)$, 1 mark for stating the merge cost.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**Q2.** State the **iteration method** for solving recurrences. What information is obtained at each iteration step?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The iteration method (also called the unfolding method) solves a recurrence by repeatedly substituting the recurrence equation into itself to obtain a *pattern* that can be summed in closed form. At each iteration step the following information is obtained:
> 1. The **work done at the current recursion level** (a term of the form $a^{i}f(n/b^{i})$).
> 2. The **recursion depth $k$**, computed by equating the sub-problem size to the base case.
> 3. The **total work** as a summation over all levels, evaluated using standard series formulas.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for naming/definition, 2 marks for listing the three pieces of information.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the base case substitution** (loses up to 2 marks): Always substitute $T(1)$ at the termination depth.
> - **Mis-applying the geometric series formula** (loses up to 3 marks): Carefully verify the common ratio $r$. If $r < 1$, the series converges to $\frac{1}{1-r}$.
> - **Skipping pattern recognition** (loses up to 2 marks): Examiners want explicit demonstration that you *see* the pattern after 2-3 iterations, not blind algebra.
> - **Forgetting to convert $\log_{b} n$ into $\Theta$ notation** (loses 1 mark): Write the final answer in $\Theta(\cdot)$ form explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q.A(a)** Solve the following recurrence using the iteration method:
$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$
Show at least three iterations and determine the asymptotic complexity $\Theta(\cdot)$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1:**
$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1$$

**Iteration 2:**
$$T(n) = 2\left[2T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 1\right] + 1 = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2 + 1$$
$$T(n) = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 3$$

**Iteration 3:**
$$T(n) = 4\left[2T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 1\right] + 3 = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 4 + 3$$
$$T(n) = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 7$$

**Pattern after $k$ iterations:**
$$T(n) = 2^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + (2^{k} - 1)$$

**Termination:** When $\frac{n}{2^{k}} = 1 \Rightarrow k = \log_{2} n$.

**Final substitution:**
$$T(n) = 2^{\log_{2} n} \cdot T(1) + (2^{\log_{2} n} - 1)$$
$$T(n) = n \cdot 1 + (n - 1) = 2n - 1$$

**Asymptotic bound:**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n)}$$

**Q.A(b)** Solve the following recurrence using the iteration method:
$$T(n) = 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$
Show the pattern, compute the summation explicitly, and state the asymptotic complexity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Iterative Expansion:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n \\
&= 3\left[3T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n}{2}\right] + n \\
&= 9T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{3n}{2} + n \\
&= 9\left[3T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + \frac{5n}{2} \\
&= 27T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{9n}{4} + \frac{5n}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pattern after $k$ iterations:**
$$T(n) = 3^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + n \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{i}$$

**Termination:** $\frac{n}{2^{k}} = 1 \Rightarrow k = \log_{2} n$.

**Summation evaluation (geometric series with $r = 3/2 > 1$):**
$$\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{i} = \frac{(3/2)^{k} - 1}{3/2 - 1} = 2\left[\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{k} - 1\right]$$
$$= 2\left[\frac{3^{k}}{2^{k}} - 1\right]$$

**Final substitution:**
$$T(n) = 3^{\log_{2} n} \cdot T(1) + 2n\left[\frac{3^{\log_{2} n}}{2^{\log_{2} n}} - 1\right]$$
$$T(n) = n^{\log_{2} 3} \cdot 1 + 2n\left[\frac{n^{\log_{2} 3}}{n} - 1\right]$$
$$T(n) = n^{\log_{2} 3} + 2n^{\log_{2} 3} - 2n$$
$$T(n) = 3 \cdot n^{\log_{2} 3} - 2n$$

**Asymptotic bound:** Since $\log_{2} 3 \approx 1.585 > 1$, the term $n^{\log_{2} 3}$ dominates $n$:
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{2} 3})}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q.B(a)** Solve the following linear recurrence using the iteration method:
$$T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$
Find the closed-form expression and the asymptotic bound. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Iterative Expansion:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n-1) + 1 \\
&= 2[2T(n-2) + 1] + 1 = 4T(n-2) + 2 + 1 \\
&= 4[2T(n-3) + 1] + 3 = 8T(n-3) + 4 + 3 \\
&= 8T(n-3) + 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pattern after $k$ iterations:**
$$T(n) = 2^{k} T(n-k) + (2^{k} - 1)$$

**Termination:** When $n - k = 1 \Rightarrow k = n - 1$.

**Final substitution:**
$$T(n) = 2^{n-1} \cdot T(1) + (2^{n-1} - 1)$$
$$T(n) = 2^{n-1} \cdot 1 + 2^{n-1} - 1$$
$$T(n) = 2^{n} - 1$$

**Asymptotic bound:**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(2^{n})}$$
This is the recurrence associated with the naive recursive computation of Fibonacci-like sequences and the Tower of Hanoi problem.

**Q.B(b)** Solve the following recurrence using the iteration method:
$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{3}\right) + T\!\left(\frac{2n}{3}\right) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$
Estimate the asymptotic complexity using the iteration method by computing the upper and lower bounds on work per level. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Structural observation:** The two sub-problems have sizes $n/3$ and $2n/3$. The larger sub-problem dominates the recursion depth.

**Termination:** The larger sub-problem ends when $\frac{2n}{3^{k}} = 1 \Rightarrow k = \log_{3/2} n$ (this is an upper bound on the recursion depth).

**Per-level work analysis:** At every level, the combine cost is $n$. The number of sub-problems may vary slightly due to asymmetric branching, but the **work per level remains $\Theta(n)$** because each sub-problem's merge cost sums to $n$.

**Upper bound on total work:**
$$T(n) \leq c \cdot n \cdot \log_{3/2} n$$
for some constant $c$.

**Lower bound:** The smaller sub-problem ends at depth $\log_{3} n$. Even accounting for the imbalance, at least $\log_{3} n$ levels of $n$-cost work occur:
$$T(n) \geq c' \cdot n \cdot \log_{3} n$$
for some constant $c'$.

**Since $\log_{3/2} n$ and $\log_{3} n$ differ only by a constant factor, both bounds are equivalent:**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$
This is the recurrence associated with algorithms like **median-of-medians selection** and certain balanced tree operations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before entering the KTU examination hall.**

- [ ] **Recurrence** = equation expressing $T(n)$ in terms of $T$ on smaller inputs + non-recursive cost.
- [ ] **Iteration method** = repeatedly substitute the recurrence into itself until a pattern emerges, then sum and apply asymptotics.
- [ ] Always **show 2–3 explicit iterations** before stating the general pattern (KTU valuation requires this).
- [ ] For divide-and-conquer $T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n)$, the **recursion depth** is $k=\log_{b} n$.
- [ ] For linear $T(n)=T(n-1)+f(n)$, the **recursion depth** is $k=n-1$ (or appropriate base case offset).
- [ ] **Geometric series with $r<1$** converges; with $r>1$ it diverges — choose formula carefully.
- [ ] **Logarithm identity $b^{\log_{b} n} = n$** must be applied at the final step.
- [ ] **Final answer MUST be in $\Theta(\cdot)$, $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$, or $\Omega(\cdot)$ notation** — not just a closed-form polynomial.
- [ ] **Merge Sort recurrence** $T(n)=2T(n/2)+n \Rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$.
- [ ] **Binary Search recurrence** $T(n)=T(n/2)+1 \Rightarrow \Theta(\log n)$.
- [ ] **Tower of Hanoi recurrence** $T(n)=2T(n-1)+1 \Rightarrow \Theta(2^{n})$.
- [ ] **Linear scan recurrence** $T(n)=T(n-1)+n \Rightarrow \Theta(n^{2})$.
- [ ] When sub-problems are **unevenly sized** (e.g., $n/3$ and $2n/3$), use the **largest sub-problem's depth** as the upper bound.
- [ ] The **iteration method proves the bound**; the Master Theorem (Module 1 next sub-topic) only **states the bound** — use iteration when the Master Theorem does not apply (e.g., irregular coefficients, non-polynomial $f(n)$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursion Tree Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT636 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS & DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recursion Tree Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Recursion Tree Method** is a *non-iterative, visually-driven asymptotic technique* used to solve recurrence relations of the form:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

where:
- **$a \geq 1$** is the branching factor (number of sub-problems per level)
- **$b > 1$** is the size-reduction factor
- **$f(n)$** is the cost of work done **outside the recursive calls** (divide + combine cost)

In this method, the recurrence is *expanded into a tree* where each node represents the cost contributed by a single sub-problem. The total running time $T(n)$ is obtained by **summing the costs across all levels** of the tree, and then applying either a geometric-series summation, induction, or the **Master Theorem** on the resulting closed-form series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Recursion Tree Method is explicitly listed under Module 1 as a primary sub-asymptotic technique alongside the **Substitution Method** and the **Master Theorem**. It is the *intuitive bridge* between these two — visually justifying why Master Theorem cases hold, and providing the structural guess needed for the Substitution Method.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **manager of a corporate pyramid scheme** (a non-illegal one — think of it like an *organizational hierarchy chart*).

1. The **CEO** (root node) receives one big project of size $n$. She splits it into **$a$ sub-projects**, each of size $n/b$, and keeps aside a small personal workload $f(n)$ for coordinating.
2. Each **Senior Manager** (next level) receives one of those sub-projects of size $n/b$. They do the same: split into $a$ smaller pieces of size $n/b^2$, plus do their own $f(n/b)$ worth of coordination work.
3. This process **recurses** until the sub-project size drops to a constant — the *leaf level*.

The **Recursion Tree Method** is simply *drawing this hierarchy on paper* and adding up the costs column-by-column (level-by-level) to find the **grand total project cost**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a tree instead of plain algebra?** Because for non-trivial recurrences (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$), pure algebraic manipulation is messy. A tree **visualizes the geometric growth/decay** of sub-problem sizes and makes summation almost trivial.

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Recursion Depth (height of tree):** $\log_b n$ levels (for perfectly balanced recurrences).
- **Number of leaves:** $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$ (by the change-of-base identity).
- **Total cost:** Sum of all node costs = cost per level × number of levels (when regular), or geometric series (when irregular).

These three metrics are the **holy trinity** for KTU board problems — every recursion tree question tests one or more of them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a Recursion Tree

The construction process follows a strict, repeatable algorithm. Memorize this sequence — KTU frequently asks for "draw the recursion tree and find the asymptotic bound."

### Step 1 — Initialize the Root
- Draw a single root node.
- Label it with the **cost of work done at the top level**: $f(n)$.
- The recursion beneath it will contribute $a \cdot T(n/b)$ as sub-problems.

### Step 2 — Branch Per Recurrence
- From each node, draw exactly **$a$ children** (sub-problems).
- Each child carries cost $f(n/b)$ if at depth 1, $f(n/b^2)$ at depth 2, and so on.
- Continue until sub-problem size **reaches $T(1)$** — this defines the leaf level.

### Step 3 — Compute Cost Per Level
- At depth $i$ (root = depth 0):
  - Number of nodes: $a^i$
  - Sub-problem size per node: $n / b^i$
  - Cost per node: $f(n / b^i)$
  - **Total cost at level $i$**: $a^i \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b^i}\right)$

### Step 4 — Sum the Levels
- Find depth $L$ where $n/b^L = \Theta(1)$, i.e., $L = \log_b n$.
- Total cost:
$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{L} a^i \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Apply Asymptotic Bounds
- Use **geometric series sums** or compare with the integral test to bound the sum.

## 2.2 The Three Strategic Outcomes

For recurrences matching the Master Theorem form, the recursion tree yields one of three patterns:

| Pattern | When It Triggers | Resulting Bound |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Root-Dominated** | $f(n)$ grows faster than the leaves $\Rightarrow f(n) = \Omega\!\left(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon}\right)$ | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ |
| **Balanced (Uniform)** | All levels have **equal cost** | $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(f(n) \log n\right)$ |
| **Leaf-Dominated** | The bottom level overwhelms the rest | $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print / memorize this table.** It is the single most-tested artifact in Module 1 recursion tree problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Engineering / Algorithmic Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Recurrence form | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | Divide-and-conquer template (Merge Sort, Binary Search, Strassen) |
| 2 | Nodes at level $i$ | $a^i$ | Sub-problem fan-out factor |
| 3 | Sub-problem size at level $i$ | $n / b^i$ | Shrinkage ratio per recursion |
| 4 | Cost per node at level $i$ | $f(n / b^i)$ | Local combine cost |
| 5 | **Total cost at level $i$** | $a^i \cdot f(n / b^i)$ | Level-wise contribution |
| 6 | Tree height (depth) | $\log_b n$ | Recursion depth until $T(1)$ |
| 7 | Number of leaves | $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$ | Base-case workload |
| 8 | Total cost (sum) | $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_b n} a^i f(n/b^i)$ | Grand total running time |
| 9 | Critical exponent | $n^{\log_b a}$ | The "comparison benchmark" against $f(n)$ |
| 10 | Geometric sum (ratio $r$) | $\sum_{i=0}^{k} r^i = \dfrac{r^{k+1}-1}{r-1}$ for $r > 1$ | Used when $f$ is constant or polynomial |

> **Critical pipe-escape rule used above:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` was replaced with `\vert` or contextual phrasing to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Method

In real-world software engineering, the recursion tree is more than an exam artifact — it is used to:

- **Predict cache performance:** Tree depth $\log_b n$ tells you the **memory-access latency** of divide-and-conquer kernels (FFT, matrix multiplication).
- **Estimate parallel speedup:** Leaves $n^{\log_b a}$ represent the *maximum degree of parallelism* on multi-core systems.
- **Detect algorithmic bottlenecks:** A root-dominated tree means *combine logic is the bottleneck* (typical in FFT's butterfly network).
- **Verify recurrence solvers:** The tree serves as a **pedagogical proof** that the Master Theorem and Akra–Bazzi method give correct results.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1 (Canonical — Merge Sort type):**
> Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ using the recursion tree method.

## 3.1 Construction of the Tree

We expand the recurrence to a depth where $n/2^L = 1$, giving $L = \log_2 n$.

```
Level 0 (root):     [   n   ]           ← cost = n, 1 node
                    /        \
Level 1:          [n/2]    [n/2]        ← cost = n/2 + n/2 = n, 2 nodes
                 /   \    /    \
Level 2:       [n/4][n/4][n/4][n/4]     ← cost = 4·(n/4) = n, 4 nodes
               ............... (continues)
```

**Observation:** At every level, the total cost is exactly $n$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.

$$a = 2, \quad b = 2, \quad f(n) = n$$

**Step 2:** Compute the critical exponent.

$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$$

**Step 3:** Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.

$$f(n) = n = n^{\log_b a}$$

This is the **Boundary Case (Master Theorem Case 2)** — the costs at all levels are equal.

**Step 4:** Compute the cost at generic level $i$.

At level $i$:
- Number of nodes = $a^i = 2^i$
- Cost per node = $f(n/b^i) = n/2^i$
- Total cost at level $i$:

$$C_i = a^i \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right) = 2^i \cdot \frac{n}{2^i} = n$$

**Step 5:** Number of levels.

$$L = \log_b n = \log_2 n$$

**Step 6:** Sum the costs across all levels plus the leaves.

The cost at every internal level is $n$. Number of internal levels is $\log_2 n$ (from level 0 to $\log_2 n - 1$).

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n \;+\; n^{\log_2 2} \cdot \Theta(1)$$

$$T(n) = n \cdot \log_2 n \;+\; n \cdot \Theta(1)$$

**Step 7:** Asymptotic bound.

$$T(n) = \Theta(n \log n) \quad \blacksquare$$

This matches Merge Sort's expected complexity — a KTU examiner's favorite verification.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 2 (Leaf-dominated — Binary Search type):**
> Solve $T(n) = T(n/2) + \Theta(1)$ using the recursion tree method.

## 3.3 Construction of the Tree

```
Level 0:     [ c ]              ← cost c, 1 node
Level 1:     [ c ]              ← cost c, 1 node
Level 2:     [ c ]              ← cost c, 1 node
  ...
Level L:     [ c ]              ← cost c, 1 node
```

**Observation:** Only **one branch per level** (since $a=1$). The tree is a single chain of length $L = \log_2 n$.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.

$$a = 1, \quad b = 2, \quad f(n) = \Theta(1) = c$$

**Step 2:** Critical exponent.

$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 1} = n^0 = 1$$

**Step 3:** Cost per level.

$$C_i = a^i \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right) = 1^i \cdot c = c$$

Each level contributes a constant $c$.

**Step 4:** Number of levels.

$$L = \log_b n = \log_2 n$$

**Step 5:** Total cost.

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n} c = c \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)$$

**Step 6:** Asymptotic bound.

$$T(n) = \Theta(\log n) \quad \blacksquare$$

This confirms Binary Search's logarithmic complexity.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 3 (Root-dominated — Dense polynomial combine):**
> Solve $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ using the recursion tree method.

## 3.5 Construction of the Tree

At level $i$:
- Nodes: $3^i$
- Cost per node: $(n/4^i)^2 = n^2 / 16^i$
- Total cost at level $i$:

$$C_i = 3^i \cdot \frac{n^2}{16^i} = n^2 \cdot \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i$$

Since $3/16 < 1$, the costs **decay geometrically**.

## 3.6 Exhaustive Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1:** Parameters.

$$a = 3, \quad b = 4, \quad f(n) = n^2$$

**Step 2:** Critical exponent.

$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$$

**Step 3:** Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$.

Since $n^2$ grows polynomially faster (with exponent $2 > 0.792$), $f(n)$ dominates.

**Step 4:** Geometric series sum.

The costs form a decreasing geometric series with first term $n^2$ and ratio $r = 3/16$.

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} n^2 \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i$$

Using the geometric series formula for $r \neq 1$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{k} r^i = \frac{1 - r^{k+1}}{1 - r}$$

Apply it:

$$T(n) = n^2 \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{3}{16}\right)^{\log_4 n}}{1 - \dfrac{3}{16}}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the tail term.

$$\left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^{\log_4 n} = n^{\log_4 (3/16)} = n^{\log_4 3 - 2}$$

Since $\log_4 3 < 2$, this term vanishes as $n \to \infty$.

**Step 6:** Asymptotic bound.

$$T(n) = n^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 3/16} = n^2 \cdot \frac{16}{13} = \Theta(n^2) \quad \blacksquare$$

This corresponds to **Master Theorem Case 3** — root-dominated.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Pattern Recognition Cheat Sheet

| Recurrence | $a$ | $b$ | $f(n)$ | $n^{\log_b a}$ | Dominant Cost | Result |
|------------|-----|-----|--------|----------------|---------------|--------|
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | 2 | 2 | $n$ | $n$ | Uniform | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | 1 | 2 | $1$ | $1$ | Uniform | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ | 3 | 4 | $n^2$ | $n^{0.79}$ | Root | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ | 4 | 2 | $n$ | $n^2$ | Leaf | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ | 2 | 2 | $n^2$ | $n$ | Root | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^2$ | 7 | 2 | $n^2$ | $n^{2.81}$ | Leaf | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0["ROOT n cost 1n"]:::root
    L1A["n/2 cost n/2"]:::lvl1
    L1B["n/2 cost n/2"]:::lvl1
    L2A["n/4 cost n/4"]:::lvl2
    L2B["n/4 cost n/4"]:::lvl2
    L2C["n/4 cost n/4"]:::lvl2
    L2D["n/4 cost n/4"]:::lvl2
    L3A["BASE cost 1"]:::leaf
    L3B["BASE cost 1"]:::leaf
    L3C["BASE cost 1"]:::leaf
    L3D["BASE cost 1"]:::leaf
    L3E["BASE cost 1"]:::leaf
    L3F["BASE cost 1"]:::leaf
    L3G["BASE cost 1"]:::leaf
    L3H["BASE cost 1"]:::leaf

    L0 --> L1A
    L0 --> L1B
    L1A --> L2A
    L1A --> L2B
    L1B --> L2C
    L1B --> L2D
    L2A --> L3A
    L2A --> L3B
    L2B --> L3C
    L2B --> L3D
    L2C --> L3E
    L2C --> L3F
    L2D --> L3G
    L2D --> L3H

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef lvl1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    classDef lvl2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
```

**Cost annotations for examiner reference:**
- Level 0: $1 \cdot n = n$
- Level 1: $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$
- Level 2: $4 \cdot (n/4) = n$
- Level $\log_2 n$: $n \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n)$

**Total:** $n \cdot \log_2 n + \Theta(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ ✓

## 4.2 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ (Root-dominated Decay)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0["ROOT cost n squared"]:::root
    A1["cost n squared over 16"]:::lvl1
    A2["cost n squared over 16"]:::lvl1
    A3["cost n squared over 16"]:::lvl1
    B1["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2
    B2["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2
    B3["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2
    B4["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2
    B5["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2
    B6["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2
    B7["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2
    B8["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2
    B9["cost n squared over 256"]:::lvl2

    R0 --> A1
    R0 --> A2
    R0 --> A3
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B4
    A2 --> B5
    A2 --> B6
    A3 --> B7
    A3 --> B8
    A3 --> B9

    classDef root fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:4px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef lvl1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef lvl2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
```

**Per-level cost annotation:**
- Level 0: $1 \cdot n^2 = n^2$
- Level 1: $3 \cdot (n/4)^2 = \dfrac{3n^2}{16}$
- Level 2: $9 \cdot (n/16)^2 = \dfrac{9n^2}{256}$
- Geometric ratio: $r = 3/16 < 1$ → **cost decays rapidly**

**Sum (geometric series bound):**
$$T(n) \leq n^2 \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i = n^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 3/16} = \frac{16}{13} \cdot n^2 = \Theta(n^2)$$

## 4.3 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ (Leaf-dominated)

```mermaid
graph TD
    N0["ROOT cost n"]:::root
    N1A["cost n over 2"]:::lvl1
    N1B["cost n over 2"]:::lvl1
    N1C["cost n over 2"]:::lvl1
    N1D["cost n over 2"]:::lvl1
    L["LEAVES total n squared cost"]:::leaf

    N0 --> N1A
    N0 --> N1B
    N0 --> N1C
    N0 --> N1D
    N1A --> L
    N1B --> L
    N1C --> L
    N1D --> L

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef lvl1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#8B008B,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Per-level cost annotation:**
- Level 0: $1 \cdot n = n$
- Level 1: $4 \cdot (n/2) = 2n$
- Level 2: $16 \cdot (n/4) = 4n$
- Level $i$: $4^i \cdot (n/2^i) = n \cdot 2^i$ → **geometric growth, ratio $r = 2$**
- Leaf level $\log_2 n$: $n \cdot 2^{\log_2 n} = n^2$

**Total:**
$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n \cdot 2^i + \Theta(n^2) = n \cdot (2^{\log_2 n} - 1) + \Theta(n^2) = \Theta(n^2)$$

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Recursion Tree Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Recurrence Tn equals a Tn over b plus f of n"]:::input
    B["Parse a, b, f of n"]:::parse
    C["Compute critical exponent n to the log base b of a"]:::critical
    D["Choose tree type: Balanced / Root-Dominated / Leaf-Dominated"]:::branch
    E["Build tree level by level"]:::build
    F["Compute level cost C sub i"]:::levelcost
    G["Sum levels using geometric series or integral test"]:::sum
    H["Apply Theta bound"]:::output
    I["Final Answer Theta of ..."]:::final

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I

    classDef input fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef parse fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef critical fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef build fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef levelcost fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#FF69B4,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFEBCD,stroke:#D2691E,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```
