# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the recursion tree method for solving recurrences. Mention its advantages over the substitution method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **recursion tree method** is a technique for solving recurrence relations of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ by representing the recursive expansion as a tree, where:
- Each **node** represents the cost contribution of a single sub-problem.
- The **root** represents $f(n)$, and its **$a$ children** represent the $a$ recursive sub-calls, each of size $n/b$.
- The total cost $T(n)$ is computed by **summing costs level-by-level** and then applying asymptotic bounds.

**Advantages over substitution method (any 2 for full marks):**
1. It provides an **intuitive visual justification** for the asymptotic bound before formal proof is attempted.
2. It directly reveals the **dominant level** of the tree (root, uniform, or leaf), making it easier to *guess* the correct $\Theta$ bound for use in the substitution method.
3. It avoids the **trial-and-error** process of guessing the right induction hypothesis.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**For the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, calculate the number of levels and the total cost at each level using a recursion tree.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$:
- **Number of levels** (height of tree): $\log_b n = \log_2 n$
- **Cost per level** $i$:
  - Number of nodes = $2^i$
  - Sub-problem size per node = $n/2^i$
  - Cost per node = $n/2^i$
  - **Total cost at level $i$** = $2^i \cdot (n/2^i) = n$
- Since the cost at each level is uniform ($n$), the total cost is $n \cdot \log_2 n$.
- **Asymptotic bound:** $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ ✓

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each module offers an internal choice between two questions. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — students answer either one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Using the recursion tree method, derive the asymptotic bound for the recurrence:
$$T(n) = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n$$
Clearly state the cost contribution of each level and the total cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the recursion tree method with the Master Theorem for solving recurrences. When would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:** $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2 — Compute critical exponent:**
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$$

**Step 3 — Cost at level $i$:**
$$C_i = a^i \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right) = 4^i \cdot \frac{n}{2^i} = n \cdot 2^i$$

**Step 4 — Tree height:**
$$L = \log_2 n$$

**Step 5 — Total cost (internal levels):**
$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n \cdot 2^i = n \cdot \frac{2^{\log_2 n} - 1}{2 - 1} = n \cdot (n - 1)$$

**Step 6 — Leaf-level cost:** $\Theta(n^{\log_2 4}) = \Theta(n^2)$

**Step 7 — Combine:**
$$T(n) = n(n-1) + \Theta(n^2) = n^2 - n + \Theta(n^2)$$

**Step 8 — Asymptotic bound:**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Aspect | Recursion Tree Method | Master Theorem |
|--------|----------------------|----------------|
| **Approach** | Visual / constructive | Direct plug-and-chug |
| **Recurrence form** | Flexible (works for irregular splits like $T(n/3) + T(2n/3)$) | Restricted to $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ |
| **Insight provided** | Shows *which level dominates* | Only gives final bound |
| **Rigorous proof** | Requires follow-up by substitution | Provides immediate, rigorous bound |
| **Computational effort** | Moderate (requires tree drawing + series sum) | Low (constant time lookup) |
| **Best for** | Discovering the bound; irregular recurrences | Standard textbook recurrences |

**Preference:** Use the **Master Theorem** for quick, clean answers on standard recurrences. Use the **recursion tree** when:
- The recurrence has an **irregular split** (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$).
- The Master Theorem's **regularity condition** fails.
- You need to **justify** an answer (it generates a guess for the substitution method).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + cn^2$ (where $c$ is a constant) using the recursion tree method. Show all level costs and arrive at a tight asymptotic bound. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A divide-and-conquer algorithm splits a problem of size $n$ into $5$ sub-problems each of size $n/3$, with $f(n) = n\log n$ combine cost. Use the recursion tree method to derive $T(n)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Parameters:** $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $f(n) = cn^2$.

**Step 2 — Cost at level $i$:**
$$C_i = a^i \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right) = 3^i \cdot c \cdot \frac{n^2}{16^i} = cn^2 \cdot \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i$$

**Step 3 — Geometric ratio:** $r = 3/16 < 1$, so the series is **decreasing**.

**Step 4 — Sum across all internal levels:**
$$T(n) \leq \sum_{i=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} cn^2 \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^i = cn^2 \cdot \frac{1 - (3/16)^{\log_4 n}}{1 - 3/16}$$

**Step 5 — Tail term evaluation:**
$$(3/16)^{\log_4 n} = n^{\log_4(3/16)} = n^{\log_4 3 - 2} \to 0 \text{ as } n \to \infty$$

**Step 6 — Final sum:**
$$T(n) \leq cn^2 \cdot \frac{1}{13/16} = \frac{16cn^2}{13}$$

**Step 7 — Asymptotic bound:**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)}$$

This is a **root-dominated** case — consistent with Master Theorem Case 3.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Parameters:** $a = 5$, $b = 3$, $f(n) = n\log n$.

**Step 2 — Critical exponent:**
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_3 5} \approx n^{1.465}$$

**Step 3 — Cost at level $i$:**
$$C_i = a^i \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right) = 5^i \cdot \frac{n}{3^i} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{n}{3^i}\right)$$

$$C_i = n \cdot \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^i \cdot \left[\log n - i \log 3\right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify growth rate:** Since $5/3 > 1$, the leading term $n \cdot (5/3)^i \cdot \log n$ **grows geometrically**.

**Step 5 — Dominant level:** The **leaf level dominates**.

**Step 6 — Leaf cost:**
$$\text{Leaf level cost} = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_3 5}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Final bound:**
$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_3 5}\right) \approx \Theta\!\left(n^{1.465}\right)}$$

This is the **leaf-dominated (Case 3 of Master Theorem)** — even though $f(n) = n \log n$ is not a polynomial beating $n^{\log_3 5}$, the multiplicative $5^i$ factor overwhelms everything except the bottom level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the leaf-level cost:** Many students sum only internal levels and omit $\Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ at the leaves. For $a > 1$ leaf-dominated cases (like $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$), this costs **3–4 marks** because the leaves dominate.
>
> 2. **Wrong tree height:** For $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, the height is **always** $\log_b n$, *not* $\log_2 n$ and *not* $n$. Using $\log_2 n$ when $b = 3$ loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing $a$ and $b$:** $a$ = number of sub-problems, $b$ = shrinkage factor. Reversing them gives the wrong critical exponent $n^{\log_b a}$.
>
> 4. **No geometric series evaluation shown:** Even if the answer is correct, KTU examiners require the **explicit summation step** (showing the formula for $\sum r^i$). Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Stating $\Theta$ without justification:** You must show *why* the lower-order terms vanish (e.g., because $r < 1$ in a decreasing geometric series).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second recap — read this on the morning of your exam:**

- **Recurrence form assumed:** $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$
- **Cost at level $i$:** $C_i = a^i \cdot f(n/b^i)$ — **memorize this formula verbatim**
- **Tree height:** $\log_b n$ — never substitute $\log_2 n$ unless $b=2$
- **Number of leaves:** $n^{\log_b a}$ — this is the *Master Theorem's critical exponent*
- **Three regimes:**
  - **Root dominates** → $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$
  - **Uniform** → $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n) \log n)$
  - **Leaf dominates** → $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$
- **Geometric series sum (for $r \neq 1$):** $\sum_{i=0}^{k} r^i = \dfrac{r^{k+1} - 1}{r - 1}$
- **Canonical examples to remember:**
  - Merge Sort: $2T(n/2) + n$ → $\Theta(n \log n)$
  - Binary Search: $T(n/2) + 1$ → $\Theta(\log n)$
  - Strassen: $7T(n/2) + n^2$ → $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$
  - Karatsuba: $3T(n/2) + n$ → $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$
- **Key pitfall:** Always include the leaf-level contribution in your final sum.
- **Big-picture takeaway:** The recursion tree is the *visual reasoning tool*; the Master Theorem is the *computational shortcut*; the substitution method is the *rigorous proof*. They form a triad in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Substitution Method and Master's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT636 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS & DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Substitution Method and Master's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Solving Recurrences?

A **recurrence relation** is an equation that defines a sequence of values recursively, where each term is expressed as a function of one or more previous terms. In Algorithm Analysis, recurrences model the **running time $T(n)$** of divide-and-conquer algorithms, where the problem of size $n$ is split into subproblems of smaller size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Recurrence relations are equations which describe functions in terms of their value on smaller inputs. Solving a recurrence means finding a **closed-form expression** $T(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ that asymptotically bounds the function.

### 1.2 The Two Pillars of Solving Recurrences

**Pillar 1 — Substitution Method (The Inductive Approach):**
The substitution method is the most general technique to bound (upper or lower) a recurrence. It consists of two key steps:
1. **Guess** the form of the solution (e.g., $T(n) = O(n \log n)$).
2. **Verify** the guess using **mathematical induction**.

**Pillar 2 — Master’s Theorem (The Cookbook Approach):**
The Master’s theorem provides a **plug-and-chug method** for solving recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, where $a \geq 1$ and $b > 1$. It expresses $T(n)$ asymptotically in terms of the comparison between $f(n)$ and $n^{\log_b a}$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Family Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Royal Family Inheritance":**
> Imagine a king with $a$ princes. He divides his kingdom of size $n$ into $b$ equal parts, gives each part to a prince, who must pay a tax $f(n)$ to the crown for managing the land. The total cost $T(n)$ is the work each prince does **plus** the tax. The Master’s Theorem tells us: *"Whichever cost grows faster — the size of the realm ($n^{\log_b a}$) or the tax burden $f(n)$ — wins the asymptotic race."* The substitution method is like an auditor checking every claim the king makes.

### 1.4 Why These Methods Matter in KTU

- **Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen’s Matrix Multiplication, Karatsuba Multiplication** — every divide-and-conquer algorithm yields a recurrence.
- KTU board exams (2019–2024) consistently ask **3-mark** derivations and **14-mark** applied problems on these theorems.
- Mastering them is the gateway to **Module 2 (Divide and Conquer)** and **Module 3 (Dynamic Programming)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Substitution Method — Step-by-Step Logic

The substitution method (also called the *induction method*) involves three logical phases:

**Phase 1 — Guess the Bound**
We guess an upper bound $O(g(n))$ for $T(n)$. The guess can come from:
- Recursion-tree intuition
- Previous experience with similar recurrences
- Empirical observation from small inputs

**Phase 2 — Substitute and Simplify**
Plug the guess into the recurrence's right-hand side. Replace $T(k)$ for $k < n$ with the induction hypothesis $c \cdot g(k)$, then simplify using algebraic identities.

**Phase 3 — Verify by Induction**
Show that for sufficiently large $n$, the inductive hypothesis holds. This usually requires identifying **boundary conditions** and finding an appropriate constant $c > 0$.

### 2.2 Subtleties of the Substitution Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtleties to Watch:**
> - **Sometimes the guess is wrong by a constant factor** — the math "almost works" but a floor/ceiling term ruins it. The fix is to *strengthen* the induction hypothesis (subtract a lower-order term).
> - **Changing variables** is a powerful trick when the recurrence has unusual structure (e.g., $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + \log n$).
> - **Boundary conditions** matter: induction must hold for all $n \geq n_0$, not just asymptotically.

### 2.3 Master’s Theorem — The Three-Case Decision Rule

Let $a \geq 1$ and $b > 1$ be constants, let $f(n)$ be an asymptotically positive function, and let $T(n)$ be defined on the nonnegative integers by the recurrence:
$$T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$$

Define the **critical exponent**:
$$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_b a}$$

Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$:

**Case 1 — $f(n)$ grows polynomially slower than $n^{\log_b a}$:**
$$f(n) = O\left(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}\right) \text{ for some constant } \epsilon > 0$$
$$T(n) = \Theta\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)$$

**Case 2 — $f(n)$ grows at the same rate as $n^{\log_b a}$ (up to a log factor):**
$$f(n) = \Theta\left(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n\right) \text{ for some constant } k \geq 0$$
$$T(n) = \Theta\left(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n\right)$$

**Case 3 — $f(n)$ grows polynomially faster than $n^{\log_b a}$:**
$$f(n) = \Omega\left(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon}\right) \text{ for some constant } \epsilon > 0$$
**AND** the *regularity condition* holds:
$$a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n) \text{ for some constant } c < 1 \text{ and all sufficiently large } n$$
$$T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU board exam preparation on this topic.

| **Recurrence Aspect** | **Formula / Condition** | **Application Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| Master theorem standard form | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$ | All divide-and-conquer algorithms |
| Critical exponent | $n^{\log_b a}$ | Determines asymptotic dominance |
| Case 1 condition | $f(n) = O\left(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}\right)$, $\epsilon > 0$ | Tree level dominates (e.g., Merge Sort) |
| Case 1 solution | $T(n) = \Theta\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)$ | Recursion tree dominated by leaves |
| Case 2 condition | $f(n) = \Theta\left(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n\right)$, $k \geq 0$ | Levels are balanced (e.g., $k=0$, classic) |
| Case 2 solution | $T(n) = \Theta\left(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n\right)$ | Every level contributes equally |
| Case 3 condition | $f(n) = \Omega\left(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon}\right)$ AND regularity $af(n/b) \leq cf(n)$, $c<1$ | Root dominates (e.g., $T(n)=aT(n/b)+n^2$) |
| Case 3 solution | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ | Recursion tree dominated by root |
| Substitution induction | $T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$, find $c > 0$, $n_0$ | Bounding any recurrence |
| Strengthened hypothesis | $T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) - d$, $d > 0$ | Fixing "almost-works" guesses |
| Change of variable | Let $n = 2^m \Rightarrow m = \log n$ | Transforms non-standard recurrences |
| Geometric series sum | $\sum_{i=0}^{k} r^i = \dfrac{r^{k+1}-1}{r-1}$ | Used in Case 1 / Case 3 proofs |
| Akra–Bazzi fallback | $T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i T(b_i n) + g(n)$ | Generalization for non-uniform splits |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Merge Sort recurrence** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ → Master Case 2 → $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$. Used in every database index, including **KTU's CSL203 Data Structures lab**.
- **Binary Search** $T(n) = T(n/2) + \Theta(1)$ → Master Case 1 → $T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$. Foundation of **search engines**.
- **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication** $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ → Master Case 1 ($n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$) → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{2.807})$. Used in **graphics processing units (GPUs)**.
- **Karatsuba Multiplication** $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ → Master Case 1 → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{1.585})$. Used in **cryptographic libraries**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Substitution Method — Exhaustive Worked Example

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$.

**Step 1: Guess the Bound**
We *guess* that $T(n) = O(n \log n)$. That is, we claim there exists $c > 0$ and $n_0$ such that $T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log n$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Step 2: Substitute the Guess**
We assume (induction hypothesis) that $T(n/2) \leq c \cdot (n/2) \log(n/2)$ and substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + n \\
&\leq 2 \cdot \left[c \cdot \frac{n}{2} \log\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)\right] + n \\
&= c \cdot n \log\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n \\
&= c \cdot n \left[\log n - \log 2\right] + n \\
&= c \cdot n \log n - c \cdot n + n \\
&= c \cdot n \log n - (c - 1) \cdot n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Analyze the Result**
We want to show $T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log n$. The substitution yields $T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log n - (c-1)n$. This is **less than or equal to** $c \cdot n \log n$ **as long as** $(c-1)n \geq 0$, i.e., $c \geq 1$.

**Step 4: Handle Boundary Conditions**
Pick $c = 2$ (any $c \geq 1$ works). For $n \geq n_0 = 2$, we can verify the base case: $T(2) = 2T(1) + 2 = 4 \leq 2 \cdot 2 \log 2 = 4$. The base case holds.

**Step 5: Conclusion by Induction**
By strong induction, $T(n) \leq 2 n \log n$ for all $n \geq 2$. Therefore:
$$T(n) = O(n \log n)$$

A symmetric argument (using $\Omega$) shows $T(n) = \Omega(n \log n)$, hence $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

### 3.2 The "Almost Works" Pitfall — Strengthening the Hypothesis

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor) + T(\lceil n/2 \rceil) + 1$.

**Naïve Guess:** $T(n) = O(n)$. Substitution yields:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + T(n/2) + 1 \\
&\leq 2 \cdot c \cdot (n/2) + 1 \\
&= cn + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **NOT** $\leq cn$. The $+1$ ruins the inductive step. We need to **strengthen** the guess.

**Strengthened Guess:** $T(n) \leq cn - d$ for some $d > 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n/2) + T(n/2) + 1 \\
&\leq 2 \cdot [c(n/2) - d] + 1 \\
&= cn - 2d + 1 \\
&\leq cn - d \quad \text{(provided } d \geq 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $T(n) \leq cn - 1$ works. Therefore $T(n) = O(n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this happens:** Floors, ceilings, and constants create residual terms that pollute the induction. Subtracting a *lower-order term* absorbs them.

---

### 3.3 Change of Variables — Detailed Transformation

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + \Theta(\log \log n)$.

**Step 1: Substitute.** Let $n = 2^m$. Then $\sqrt{n} = 2^{m/2}$, so:
$$T(2^m) = T(2^{m/2}) + \Theta(\log m)$$

**Step 2: Rename.** Let $S(m) = T(2^m)$. The recurrence becomes:
$$S(m) = S(m/2) + \Theta(\log m)$$

**Step 3: Solve.** This matches Master Case 2 with $a=1$, $b=2$, $f(m) = \log m$. Critical exponent $m^{\log_2 1} = m^0 = 1$. Since $\log m = \Theta(m^0 \log^1 m)$, we are in Case 2 with $k=1$:

$$S(m) = \Theta(\log^2 m)$$

**Step 4: Back-substitute.** Since $m = \log n$, we have:
$$T(n) = S(\log n) = \Theta(\log^2 \log n)$$

---

### 3.4 Master’s Theorem — Exhaustive Case-by-Case Application

#### 3.4.1 Case 1 Worked Example (Merge Sort)

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$

**Identification:**
- $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$
- Critical exponent: $n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$

**Comparison:**
$f(n) = n$ versus $n^{\log_b a} = n^1$.
Since $n = \Theta(n)$, we check Case 2 first... wait, let us verify Case 1: Is $n = O(n^{1-\epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$? **No.** Is $n = \Omega(n^{1+\epsilon})$? **No.** So we are precisely in **Case 2** with $k=0$.

**Conclusion (Case 2 with $k=0$):**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 2} \log^1 n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

#### 3.4.2 Case 1 Worked Example (Karatsuba)

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$

**Identification:**
- $a = 3$, $b = 2$
- Critical exponent: $n^{\log_2 3} \approx n^{1.585}$

**Comparison:**
$f(n) = n$ versus $n^{1.585}$.
Since $n = O(n^{1.585 - 0.585})$, with $\epsilon = 0.585$, we are in **Case 1**.

**Conclusion:**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) = \Theta(n^{1.585})$$

#### 3.4.3 Case 3 Worked Example

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$

**Identification:**
- $a = 3$, $b = 4$
- Critical exponent: $n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$

**Comparison:**
$f(n) = n^2$ versus $n^{0.792}$.
Since $n^2 = \Omega(n^{0.792 + \epsilon})$ for $\epsilon = 1 > 0$, we are in **Case 3** *provided the regularity condition holds*.

**Regularity Check:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
a \cdot f(n/b) &= 3 \cdot (n/4)^2 \\
&= 3 \cdot \frac{n^2}{16} \\
&= \frac{3}{16} n^2 \\
&\leq \frac{1}{4} \cdot n^2 \quad (\text{since } \frac{3}{16} < \frac{1}{4})
\end{aligned}
$$

So $c = 1/4 < 1$. Regularity holds.

**Conclusion:**
$$T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n^2)$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

Use this matrix to instantly classify recurrences in the KTU exam:

| **Recurrence** | $a$ | $b$ | $n^{\log_b a}$ | $f(n)$ | **Case** | **Solution** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$ | 9 | 3 | $n^2$ | $n$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $T(n) = T(2n/3) + 1$ | 1 | 3/2 | $n^0 = 1$ | $1$ | 2 ($k=0$) | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n \log n$ | 3 | 4 | $n^{0.79}$ | $n \log n$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^{0.79})$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ | 2 | 2 | $n$ | $n \log n$ | 1 | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n/\log n$ | 2 | 2 | $n$ | $n/\log n$ | 1 | $\Theta(n)$ |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ | 4 | 2 | $n^2$ | $n^2$ | 2 ($k=0$) | $\Theta(n^2 \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2 \log n$ | 4 | 2 | $n^2$ | $n^2 \log n$ | 2 ($k=1$) | $\Theta(n^2 \log^2 n)$ |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^3$ | 4 | 2 | $n^2$ | $n^3$ | 3 | $\Theta(n^3)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gap in Master’s Theorem:** Recurrences like $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n/\log n$ sit *between* Cases 1 and 2 because $n/\log n$ is *slower* than $n$ but *faster* than $n^{1-\epsilon}$ for every $\epsilon$. **Master’s Theorem does not apply.** Use the Akra–Bazzi method or recursion-tree analysis instead.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master’s Theorem — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)"] --> B["Compute critical exponent: n^log_b a"]
    B --> C{"f(n) = O(n^log_b a - epsilon)?"}
    C -- "Yes (epsilon > 0)" --> D["CASE 1: T(n) = Theta(n^log_b a)"]
    C -- "No" --> E{"f(n) = Theta(n^log_b a * log^k n)?"}
    E -- "Yes (k >= 0)" --> F["CASE 2: T(n) = Theta(n^log_b a * log^k+1 n)"]
    E -- "No" --> G{"f(n) = Omega(n^log_b a + epsilon)?"}
    G -- "No" --> H["GAP: Master Theorem NOT applicable"]
    G -- "Yes" --> I{"Regularity: a*f(n/b) <= c*f(n), c<1?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["CASE 3: T(n) = Theta(f(n))"]
    I -- "No" --> K["GAP: Regularity fails"]
```

### 4.2 Substitution Method — Procedural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Phase 1: GUESS the form T(n) = O(g(n))"] --> P2["Phase 2: SUBSTITUTE induction hypothesis"]
    P2 --> P3{"Simplified result <= c*g(n)?"}
    P3 -- "Yes" --> P4["Phase 3: VERIFY base cases and constants"]
    P4 --> P5["DONE: T(n) = O(g(n)) proven"]
    P3 -- "No (off by constant)" --> P6["Strengthen hypothesis: g(n) - d"]
    P6 --> P2
    P3 -- "No (wildly wrong)" --> P7["Re-evaluate guess via recursion tree"]
    P7 --> P1
```

### 4.3 Recursion Tree — Merge Sort Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["Level 0: T(n) — cost cn at root"]
    L1a["Level 1 Left: T(n/2) — cost cn/2"]
    L1b["Level 1 Right: T(n/2) — cost cn/2"]
    L2a["Level 2: T(n/4)"]
    L2b["Level 2: T(n/4)"]
    L2c["Level 2: T(n/4)"]
    L2d["Level 2: T(n/4)"]
    L3["...continues log n levels..."]
    LN["Leaves: n subproblems of size 1 — cost c*n*1"]

    L0 --> L1a
    L0 --> L1b
    L1a --> L2a
    L1a --> L2b
    L1b --> L2c
    L1b --> L2d
    L2a --> L3
    L2b --> L3
    L2c --> L3
    L2d --> L3
    L3 --> LN
```

### 4.4 Comparison of Dominant Costs Across Cases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CASE1["Case 1: Leaves Dominate"]
        C1A["n^log_b a much larger than f(n)"]
        C1B["Solution: T(n) = Theta(n^log_b a)"]
        C1A --> C1B
    end
    subgraph CASE2["Case 2: Balanced Levels"]
        C2A["f(n) roughly equals n^log_b a * log^k n"]
        C2B["Solution: T(n) = Theta(n^log_b a * log^k+1 n)"]
        C2A --> C2B
    end
    subgraph CASE3["Case 3: Root Dominates"]
        C3A["f(n) much larger than n^log_b a"]
        C3B["Solution: T(n) = Theta(f(n))"]
        C3A --> C3B
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**Q1.** Define a recurrence relation. Mention any two methods for solving recurrences.

**Model Answer:**
A recurrence relation is an equation that expresses each term of a sequence as a function of one or more previous terms. In algorithm analysis, it represents the running time $T(n)$ of a recursive algorithm. Two standard methods for solving recurrences are:
1. **Substitution Method** — Guess a bound and verify by mathematical induction.
2. **Master’s Theorem** — A cookbook technique for recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**Q2.** State the regularity condition in Case 3 of the Master’s theorem. Why is it necessary?

**Model Answer:**
The regularity condition states that for constants $a \geq 1$ and $b > 1$, there exists a constant $c$ with $0 < c < 1$ such that for sufficiently large $n$:
$$a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$$

It is necessary because Case 3 requires $f(n)$ to grow polynomially faster than $n^{\log_b a}$, but this alone is not sufficient — the polynomial growth must be **smooth enough** that the recursive subproblems do not collectively overwhelm the root cost. The regularity condition formalizes this smoothness requirement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$ using the Master’s theorem. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the substitution method, prove that the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ with $T(1) = 1$ satisfies $T(n) = O(2^n)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
$a = 9$, $b = 3$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2: Compute the critical exponent.**
$n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_3 9} = n^2$.

**Step 3: Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.**
$f(n) = n$ and $n^{\log_b a} = n^2$.
Since $n = O(n^{2 - \epsilon})$ for $\epsilon = 1$, Case 1 applies.

**Step 4: Apply Case 1.**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_3 9}) = \Theta(n^2)$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Guess:** $T(n) \leq c \cdot 2^n$ for some constant $c > 0$ and $n \geq n_0$.

**Induction Hypothesis:** Assume $T(n-1) \leq c \cdot 2^{n-1}$.

**Substitution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n-1) + 1 \\
&\leq 2 \cdot c \cdot 2^{n-1} + 1 \\
&= c \cdot 2^n + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

We need $T(n) \leq c \cdot 2^n$, but we got $c \cdot 2^n + 1$. This is the **"off-by-a-constant"** problem. Strengthen the hypothesis:

**Strengthened Guess:** $T(n) \leq c \cdot 2^n - 1$.

**Substitution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n-1) + 1 \\
&\leq 2(c \cdot 2^{n-1} - 1) + 1 \\
&= c \cdot 2^n - 2 + 1 \\
&= c \cdot 2^n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Base Case:** $T(1) = 1 \leq c \cdot 2 - 1 = 2c - 1$. Choose $c = 1$, then $1 \leq 1$. ✓

**Conclusion:** By induction, $T(n) \leq 2^n - 1$ for all $n \geq 1$, so $T(n) = O(2^n)$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Use the Master’s theorem to determine the asymptotic solution of $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2 \log n$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve $T(n) = 2T(\sqrt{n}) + \log n$ using the change-of-variable technique. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Parameters:** $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2 \log n$.

**Critical exponent:** $n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$.

**Comparison:** $f(n) = n^2 \log n = n^2 \cdot \log^1 n = \Theta(n^2 \log^1 n)$.
With $n^{\log_b a} = n^2$, we have $f(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^1 n)$. This matches **Case 2** with $k = 1$.

**Apply Case 2:**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^2 n)$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Substitute.** Let $n = 2^m$, so $\sqrt{n} = 2^{m/2}$, and $\log n = m$.
$$T(2^m) = 2T(2^{m/2}) + m$$

**Step 2: Rename.** Let $S(m) = T(2^m)$:
$$S(m) = 2S(m/2) + m$$

**Step 3: Apply Master’s theorem to $S(m)$.**
$a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(m) = m$.
$n^{\log_b a} = m^{\log_2 2} = m$.
Since $f(m) = m = \Theta(m \log^0 m)$, this is **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

$$S(m) = \Theta(m \log m)$$

**Step 4: Back-substitute.**
Since $m = \log n$:
$$T(n) = S(\log n) = \Theta(\log n \cdot \log \log n)$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the regularity condition** in Case 3. Just showing $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ is **not sufficient** — you must explicitly verify $a f(n/b) \leq c f(n)$ for some $c < 1$. Many students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Confusing $\log_b a$ with $\log a / \log b$**. Both forms are acceptable, but write the full expression $n^{\log_b a}$ to avoid ambiguity.
> 3. **Off-by-one in substitution**: When you strengthen the hypothesis, the strength must be enough to absorb the residual. Showing $T(n) \leq cn - 1$ but needing $T(n) \leq cn - 2$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting base cases**: Induction without a base case is incomplete. Always check $T(1)$ or $T(n_0)$.
> 5. **Applying Master’s theorem to inapplicable recurrences**: If the recurrence has $\sqrt{n}$, $n \log n$ on the right-hand side outside the recursion, or if $a$ is not constant, Master’s theorem does **not** apply. Use Akra–Bazzi or recursion-tree instead.
> 6. **Misclassifying Case 2**: If $f(n) = n^{\log_b a} \log^k n$, students often write $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ instead of $\log^{k+1} n$. The exponent increments by exactly **1**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] A **recurrence** models the cost of a recursive algorithm; solving it means finding a closed-form $\Theta$-bound.
- [ ] The **substitution method** has three phases: **Guess → Substitute → Verify by Induction**.
- [ ] When the substitution yields an extra $+1$ or $+c$, **strengthen** the hypothesis by subtracting a lower-order term.
- [ ] **Master’s theorem** applies only to recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$.
- [ ] The **critical exponent** is $n^{\log_b a}$ — this is the cost of the leaves of the recursion tree.
- [ ] **Case 1**: Leaves dominate → $f(n)$ grows polynomially slower → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- [ ] **Case 2**: Balanced → $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$. Note the **$k \to k+1$**.
- [ ] **Case 3**: Root dominates → $f(n)$ grows polynomially faster **AND** regularity holds → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
- [ ] The **regularity condition** $a f(n/b) \leq c f(n)$ is **mandatory** for Case 3.
- [ ] **Change of variable** handles recurrences with $\sqrt{n}$, $\log \log n$, etc.
- [ ] Master’s theorem has **gaps** — for $f(n)$ between $n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}$ and $n^{\log_b a}$, the theorem does not apply.
- [ ] Always **state the case** explicitly, **show the comparison**, and **justify the regularity condition** in your solution.
- [ ] Practice the **Analytical Decision Matrix** (Section 3.5) until you can classify any recurrence in under 30 seconds.
- [ ] For KTU 14-mark questions, present **complete arithmetic** — no skipping of algebraic steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self-Balancing Trees - AVL Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT636 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS & DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Self-Balancing Trees - AVL Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **AVL Tree** (Adelson-Velsky and Landis Tree) is a self-balancing **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in which the **heights of the two child subtrees of any node differ by at most one**. If at any time the difference exceeds one, rebalancing is performed to restore this property.

Formally, for every node $v$ in an AVL tree, the following **Height-Balance Property** must hold:

$$\vert h_L(v) - h_R(v) \vert \leq 1$$

where $h_L(v)$ and $h_R(v)$ are the heights of the left and right subtrees rooted at node $v$, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Balance Factor (BF)** of a node is defined as the difference between the height of the left subtree and the height of the right subtree:
>
> $$BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$$
>
> For an AVL tree, **BF(v) ∈ {-1, 0, +1}** for every node $v$. Any node with a balance factor of **+2** or **-2** is said to be **imbalanced** and triggers a **rotation**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AVL trees guarantee $O(\log n)$ time complexity for Search, Insert, and Delete operations in the worst case, making them superior to ordinary BSTs which degrade to $O(n)$ for skewed inputs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **perfectly balanced weighing scale (a balance used in old markets)**. The scale works correctly only if the weights on both pans are nearly equal. If you keep adding heavy weights to one side, the scale tilts and becomes useless for precise measurement. To fix it, you must **rearrange (rotate)** some weights until balance is restored.

In the same way:
- An **ordinary BST** can become heavily "tilted" (skewed) like a chain if elements are inserted in sorted order — making search as slow as scanning a linked list.
- An **AVL tree** constantly monitors the "tilt" (balance factor) at every node. The moment any node becomes too "tilted" (BF = ±2), the tree **automatically restructures** itself through **rotations**, much like a vigilant merchant restoring balance to the scale.

> [!TIP]  
> **Key Intuition:** The AVL tree is to a BST what an **automatic suspension system** is to a car — it constantly senses imbalance and self-corrects to maintain smooth (logarithmic) performance.

---

## 1.3 Why AVL Trees Matter (Engineering Motivation)

| Application Area | Role of AVL Tree |
|------------------|------------------|
| **Database Indexing** | Provides guaranteed $O(\log n)$ lookups for B-tree secondary indices |
| **In-Memory Dictionaries** | Used in language runtime symbol tables (e.g., early C++ STL `map` implementations) |
| **Network Routing** | Maintains balanced routing tables for fast prefix lookups |
| **Compiler Design** | Stores variable/symbol tables for rapid scope resolution |
| **Computational Geometry** | Supports interval trees and segment trees that require balance |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Physical / Performance Constants:** The constant **1.44** in the AVL height bound $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$ is a well-known empirical metric representing the worst-case height-to-log-base-2 ratio of an AVL tree. This is **strictly less than 1.5**, ensuring AVL trees are more rigidly balanced than Red-Black trees.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### Step 1: Insertion (Standard BST Insertion)
- A new key is inserted at the appropriate leaf position following standard **BST insertion rules** (left child if smaller, right child if larger).

### Step 2: Height Update
- After insertion, traverse **back up the path** from the inserted node to the root.
- Update the height of every ancestor node: $h(v) = 1 + \max(h_L(v), h_R(v))$.

### Step 3: Balance Factor Computation
- At each ancestor, compute $BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$.
- If $BF(v) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$, the tree remains balanced. Continue upward.

### Step 4: Rebalancing via Rotations
- If any node $v$ has $BF(v) = +2$ or $BF(v) = -2$, the tree is **imbalanced**.
- Identify the rotation type based on the position of the inserted node relative to $v$.

---

## 2.2 The Four AVL Rotation Cases

| Case | Imbalance Direction | Insertion Location | Rotation Applied | After BF |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------|----------|
| **LL** (Left-Left) | $BF = +2$ | Left subtree of left child | Single Right Rotation | Restored |
| **RR** (Right-Right) | $BF = -2$ | Right subtree of right child | Single Left Rotation | Restored |
| **LR** (Left-Right) | $BF = +2$ | Right subtree of left child | Left then Right Rotation | Restored |
| **RL** (Right-Left) | $BF = -2$ | Left subtree of right child | Right then Left Rotation | Restored |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** "The **first letter** tells where the imbalance is; the **second letter** tells where the new node went." The rotation always moves the deeper grandchild upward.

### Step 5: Termination
- After at most **one or two rotations**, the AVL property is restored at the imbalanced node, and the rebalancing propagates no further. The amortized cost of rebalancing per insertion is $O(1)$.

---

## 2.3 Deletion Considerations

Deletion is **more complex** than insertion because rebalancing may cascade up to the root, potentially requiring up to $O(\log n)$ rotations. The procedure is:
1. Perform standard BST deletion.
2. Update heights and balance factors along the path from the deleted node's parent to the root.
3. Perform rotation at the **first imbalanced ancestor** encountered.
4. Continue checking ancestors upward (cascading rotations may be needed).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Remarks |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------|
| 1 | **Balance Factor** | $BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$ | Must lie in $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ |
| 2 | **Node Height** | $h(v) = 1 + \max(h_L(v), h_R(v))$ | Leaf node has height 0 (or 1 by convention) |
| 3 | **Valid Range of BF** | $-1 \leq BF(v) \leq +1$ | Strict AVL invariant |
| 4 | **Imbalance Trigger** | $\vert BF(v) \vert = 2$ | Requires rotation |
| 5 | **Min Nodes Recurrence** | $N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1$ | Fibonacci-like with $N(0)=1, N(1)=2$ |
| 6 | **Height Upper Bound** | $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328$ | Tight asymptotic bound |
| 7 | **Height Lower Bound** | $h \geq \log_2(n+1)$ | Same as perfect BST |
| 8 | **Time Complexity (All Ops)** | $O(\log n)$ | Search, Insert, Delete, Find-Min, Find-Max |
| 9 | **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ | One node per element + 2 child pointers + height field |
| 10 | **Rotations per Insertion** | $\leq 2$ | Single or double rotation |
| 11 | **Rotations per Deletion** | $\leq O(\log n)$ | Cascading possible |
| 12 | **Worst-Case Search** | $O(h) = O(\log n)$ | Guaranteed logarithmic |

> [!TIP]  
> For KTU derivations, remember that the constants in Formula 6 are **empirically derived** and often appear in 2-mark short questions. Commit the value **1.44** to memory.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Systems:** AVL trees were historically used in memory-resident database indices (e.g., MySQL's in-memory hash join buffers). Modern systems prefer B-trees for disk-resident data but use AVL for cache-friendly in-memory structures.
- **Computational Geometry:** Used as the underlying structure for **interval trees**, **priority search trees**, and **range trees**.
- **Network Routers:** The **patricia trie** used in IPv4 routing uses AVL-like balancing for the internal node structure.
- **Real-Time Systems:** Because AVL trees have a **worst-case** $O(\log n)$ bound (not just average-case), they are preferred in hard real-time systems where predictable response time is critical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Number of Nodes in an AVL Tree of Height h

Let $N(h)$ denote the **minimum number of nodes** in an AVL tree of height $h$. The recurrence is derived as follows:

### Step 1: Base Cases
- A tree of height **0** (a single leaf node) has $N(0) = 1$.
- A tree of height **1** (root with one child) has $N(1) = 2$.

### Step 2: Recursive Case
For height $h \geq 2$, the most "sparse" AVL tree is built by:
- Making one subtree have height $h-1$ (the larger height).
- Making the other subtree have height $h-2$ (since the balance factor must be exactly 1 to minimize nodes).
- Including the root node itself.

Therefore:

$$N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$$

### Step 3: Solving the Recurrence
This is a Fibonacci-like linear recurrence. Subtract $N(h-1)$ from $N(h)$:

$$N(h) - N(h-1) = N(h-2)$$

Let $F(h) = N(h) + 1$. Then:

$$F(h) = N(h) + 1 = (1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)) + 1$$

$$F(h) = (N(h-1) + 1) + (N(h-2) + 1) = F(h-1) + F(h-2)$$

This is the **Fibonacci recurrence** with $F(0) = 2$ and $F(1) = 3$.

### Step 4: Closed-Form (Binet's Formula)

$$F(h) = \frac{\varphi^{h+2} - \psi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}}$$

where $\varphi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ (golden ratio) and $\psi = \frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx -0.618$.

### Step 5: Asymptotic Simplification
Since $\vert \psi \vert < 1$, the term $\psi^{h+2}$ becomes negligible for large $h$:

$$F(h) \approx \frac{\varphi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}}$$

Thus:

$$N(h) \approx \frac{\varphi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} - 1$$

### Step 6: Solving for h in terms of N
Taking logarithms:

$$h + 2 \approx \log_\varphi(N \cdot \sqrt{5})$$

$$h \approx \log_\varphi(N) + \log_\varphi(\sqrt{5}) - 2$$

Converting to base 2 using $\log_\varphi(x) = \frac{\log_2(x)}{\log_2(\varphi)}$ and noting $\log_2(\varphi) \approx 0.694$:

$$h \leq 1.4404 \cdot \log_2(N+2) - 0.3277$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The constant **1.44** comes from $\frac{1}{\log_2(\varphi)} = \frac{1}{0.694} \approx 1.44$. This explains why AVL trees guarantee $h = O(\log n)$ with a small constant factor.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Step-by-Step AVL Insertion

**Insert the sequence: 30, 20, 40, 10, 25, 50, 5** into an empty AVL tree.

### Step 1: Insert 30
- Tree: `30` → Height 0, BF(30) = 0. Balanced.

### Step 2: Insert 20
- Place 20 as left child of 30.  
- Tree: `30(0)` with left `20(0)`.  
- Heights: $h(20)=0$, $h(\text{right of 30})=−1 \equiv 0$ (convention).  
- $BF(30) = 0 - 0 = 0$. Balanced.

### Step 3: Insert 40
- Place 40 as right child of 30.  
- $BF(30) = 0$. Balanced.  
- Tree: `30(0)`, with `20(0)` left and `40(0)` right.

### Step 4: Insert 10
- Place 10 as left child of 20.  
- Heights: $h(10)=0$, $h(20)=1$, $h(40)=0$, $h(30)=2$.  
- $BF(30) = h(20) - h(40) = 1 - 0 = 1$. Balanced (within ±1).

### Step 5: Insert 25
- 25 > 20 → right of 20.  
- Heights update: $h(20)=1$, $h(30)=2$.  
- $BF(30) = 1 - 0 = 1$. Balanced.

### Step 6: Insert 50
- 50 > 30 → right of 30; 50 > 40 → right of 40.  
- Heights: $h(40)=1$, $h(30)=2$, $h(20)=1$.  
- $BF(30) = 1 - 1 = 0$. Balanced.

### Step 7: Insert 5
- 5 < 30 → left of 30; 5 < 20 → left of 20; 5 < 10 → left of 10.  
- Heights update: $h(10)=1$, $h(20)=2$, $h(30)=3$.  
- $BF(30) = h(20) - h(40) = 2 - 1 = +2$. **IMBALANCED!**

**Identify rotation:** The imbalance is at node 30 with $BF = +2$. The left child is 20 with $BF(20) = h(10) - h(25) = 1 - 0 = +1$. Since the inserted node (5) is in the **left-left** path, this is an **LL case** requiring a **Single Right Rotation**.

**Right Rotation around 30:**
- New root becomes 20.
- 30 becomes right child of 20.
- 25 (former right child of 20) becomes left child of 30.

**Final Tree:**

```
          20 (BF=0)
         /  \
       10    30 (BF=0)
       /    /  \
      5    25   40
                \
                50
```

> [!NOTE]  
> After one rotation, the AVL property is restored globally. No further rebalancing is needed.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Rotation

| Scenario | Node v's BF | Child c's BF | Inserted in | Case | Action |
|----------|-------------|--------------|-------------|------|--------|
| 1 | $+2$ | $+1$ | Left of Left | **LL** | Right Rotate(v) |
| 2 | $-2$ | $-1$ | Right of Right | **RR** | Left Rotate(v) |
| 3 | $+2$ | $-1$ | Right of Left | **LR** | Left Rotate(c); Right Rotate(v) |
| 4 | $-2$ | $+1$ | Left of Right | **RL** | Right Rotate(c); Left Rotate(v) |

> [!TIP]  
> **Decision rule for KTU exams:** Look at the **child's balance factor**, not the imbalance location. If child BF matches the sign of imbalance → **single rotation**; if opposite sign → **double rotation**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AVL Tree: Before and After LL Rotation (Visual)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates an LL imbalance case and its resolution via a single right rotation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph BEFORE_ROT["BEFORE LL Right Rotation"]
        A1[Node 30 BF plus 2] --> B1[Node 20 BF plus 1]
        A1 --> C1[Node 40 BF 0]
        B1 --> D1[Node 10 BF 0]
        B1 --> E1[Node 25 BF 0]
        D1 --> F1[Node 5 BF 0 NEW NODE]
    end

    subgraph AFTER_ROT["AFTER LL Right Rotation at 30"]
        A2[Node 20 BF 0 NEW ROOT] --> B2[Node 10 BF plus 1]
        A2 --> C2[Node 30 BF 0]
        B2 --> D2[Node 5 BF 0]
        C2 --> E2[Node 25 BF 0]
        C2 --> F2[Node 40 BF 0]
        F2 --> G2[Node 50 BF 0]
    end

    BEFORE_ROT -.TRANSFORM.-> AFTER_ROT
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AVL Insertion Pipeline

The diagram below maps the complete functional flow of an AVL insertion operation, including the rebalancing decision tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start: New Key K]) --> BSTINSERT[Perform Standard BST Insertion of K]
    BSTINSERT --> UPDHEIGHT[Update Heights of Ancestors from K to Root]
    UPDHEIGHT --> COMPUTEBF[Compute Balance Factor at Each Ancestor]
    COMPUTEBF --> CHECKBF{Is BF within minus 1 to plus 1?}
    CHECKBF -- Yes --> NEXTNODE{More Ancestors?}
    CHECKBF -- No --> IDENTIFY[Identify Imbalanced Node V]
    NEXTNODE -- Yes --> COMPUTEBF
    NEXTNODE -- No --> DONE([Insertion Complete Tree is Balanced])
    IDENTIFY --> CHECKCHILD{What is BF of Child C of V?}
    CHECKCHILD -- plus 1 and V is plus 2 --> LLR[Apply Single Right Rotation LL Case]
    CHECKCHILD -- minus 1 and V is minus 2 --> RRL[Apply Single Left Rotation RR Case]
    CHECKCHILD -- minus 1 and V is plus 2 --> LRL[Apply LR Double Rotation Left then Right]
    CHECKCHILD -- plus 1 and V is minus 2 --> RLL[Apply RL Double Rotation Right then Left]
    LLR --> UPDHEIGHT2[Update Heights After Rotation]
    RRL --> UPDHEIGHT2
    LRL --> UPDHEIGHT2
    RLL --> UPDHEIGHT2
    UPDHEIGHT2 --> DONE
```

---

## 4.3 Rotation Mechanics — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LL_BEFORE["LL Before"]
        LL1[Z] --> LL2[Y]
        LL2 --> LL3[X]
    end

    subgraph LL_AFTER["LL After Right Rotate"]
        LR1[Y] --> LR2[X]
        LR1 --> LR3[Z]
    end

    LL_BEFORE -.RIGHT ROTATE.-> LL_AFTER
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RR_BEFORE["RR Before"]
        RR1[X] --> RR2[Y]
        RR1 --> RR3[Z]
    end

    subgraph RR_AFTER["RR After Left Rotate"]
        RL1[Y] --> RL2[X]
        RL1 --> RL3[Z]
    end

    RR_BEFORE -.LEFT ROTATE.-> RR_AFTER
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the schematic:** In the LL case, $Y$ is the imbalanced node, $X$ is the left child, and $Z$ (the new node) is in the left subtree of $X$. After right rotation, $X$ becomes the new subtree root, with $Y$ as its right child.

---

## 4.4 AVL Tree Height vs Node Count (Theoretical Curve)

```mermaid
graph LR
    H0[h 0 N 1] --> H1[h 1 N 2]
    H1 --> H2[h 2 N 4]
    H2 --> H3[h 3 N 7]
    H3 --> H4[h 4 N 12]
    H4 --> H5[h 5 N 20]
    H5 --> H6[h 6 N 33]
    H6 --> H7[h 7 N 54]
    H7 --> H8[h 8 N 88]
    H8 --> H9[h 9 N 143]
    H9 --> H10[h 10 N 232]
```

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how the node count grows **exponentially** (Fibonacci-like) as height increases. This means a height increase of just **1** can absorb a large number of additional nodes — which is exactly why AVL trees stay short (height $\approx 1.44 \log_2 N$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an AVL tree. What is the balance factor of a node? Mention the conditions under which an AVL tree is considered to be balanced.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **AVL tree** is a height-balanced binary search tree in which, for every node, the difference between the heights of the left and right subtrees (called the **balance factor**) is at most **1**.

The **balance factor** of a node $v$ is defined as:

$$BF(v) = h_{left}(v) - h_{right}(v)$$

An AVL tree is considered balanced if, for every node $v$ in the tree:

$$BF(v) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$$

If any node has $BF(v) = +2$ or $BF(v) = -2$, the tree becomes imbalanced and must be rebalanced through rotations. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for balance factor, 1 for condition)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four types of rotations used to rebalance an AVL tree. Identify the imbalance pattern for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Rotation Type | Imbalance Pattern | Operation |
|---|---------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **LL Rotation** (Right Rotation) | Node inserted in **Left subtree of Left child** of the imbalanced node | Single right rotation at imbalanced node |
| 2 | **RR Rotation** (Left Rotation) | Node inserted in **Right subtree of Right child** | Single left rotation at imbalanced node |
| 3 | **LR Rotation** | Node inserted in **Right subtree of Left child** | Left rotation on child, then right rotation on node |
| 4 | **RL Rotation** | Node inserted in **Left subtree of Right child** | Right rotation on child, then left rotation on node |

**(3 marks: 0.5 per rotation type + 0.5 per imbalance pattern + 0.5 for grouped table presentation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Construct an AVL tree by inserting the following elements in the given order: **50, 20, 70, 10, 30, 25, 40, 60, 80, 35**. Show the tree after every insertion and clearly mention any rotations performed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Insert 50:** Tree: `50` — Balanced. ✓

**Insert 20:** `50(0)` with left child `20(0)` — Balanced. ✓

**Insert 70:** `50(0)` with children `20(0)` and `70(0)` — Balanced. ✓

**Insert 10:** Heights: $h(10)=0, h(20)=1, h(50)=2$. $BF(50) = 1$. Balanced. ✓

**Insert 30:** 30 > 20 → right of 20. $h(20)=1$. $BF(50) = 1-0=1$. Balanced. ✓

**Insert 25:** 25 < 30 → left of 30. $h(20)=2, h(50)=3$. $BF(50) = 2-0 = +2$ → **IMBALANCED!**

**Identify case:** 50 has $BF=+2$, left child 20 has $BF(20) = h(10) - h(30) = 0 - 1 = -1$. This is an **LR case** (since the new node 25 went into the right subtree of the left child).

**LR Rotation at 50:**
- Step 1: Left rotate around 20. 30 becomes the new left child of 50; 25 remains left of 30; 20 becomes left child of 30.
- Step 2: Right rotate around 50. 30 becomes the new root; 20 is its left child, 50 is its right child.

**Tree after insertion 25 (after LR rotation):**
```
        30
       /  \
      20   50
     /    /  \
    10   40   70
        /
       25
```

**Insert 40:** 40 < 50 → left of 50. $h(50)=2, h(30)=3$. $BF(30) = 1-2 = -1$. Balanced. ✓

**Insert 60:** 60 > 50 → right of 50. $h(50)=2$. $BF(30) = -1$. Balanced. ✓

**Insert 80:** 80 > 70 → right of 70. $h(70)=1, h(50)=2$. $BF(30) = -1$. Balanced. ✓

**Insert 35:** 35 < 50 → left of 50; 35 < 40 → left of 40. $h(40)=1, h(50)=2, h(30)=3$. $BF(30) = h(20) - h(50) = 1 - 2 = -1$. Balanced. ✓

**Final AVL Tree:**
```
            30
           /  \
         20    50
         /    /  \
        10   40   70
            /    / \
           25   60  80
          /
         35? -- (Actually 35 is left of 40)
```

Corrected final tree:
```
              30
             /  \
           20    50
           /    /  \
          10   40   70
              /    /  \
             25  60   80
             /
            35
```
All balance factors are within $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. **(7 marks: 0.5 per insertion + 1 for rotation identification + 1 for correct rotation)**

---

**(b)** For an AVL tree of height $h$, derive the recurrence relation for the minimum number of nodes $N(h)$. Show that $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328$ where $n$ is the number of nodes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

Let $N(h)$ = minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $h$.

**Base cases:**
- $N(0) = 1$ (single node)
- $N(1) = 2$ (root + one child)

**Recurrence:** For $h \geq 2$, the sparsest AVL tree of height $h$ has its root, one subtree of height $h-1$, and the other of height $h-2$ (to minimize nodes while maintaining the balance property):

$$N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$$

**Solving:** Let $F(h) = N(h) + 1$. Then $F(h) = F(h-1) + F(h-2)$ with $F(0) = 2, F(1) = 3$.

By Binet's formula with $\varphi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$:

$$F(h) = \frac{\varphi^{h+2} - \psi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}}$$

Since $\vert \psi \vert < 1$, for large $h$:

$$F(h) \approx \frac{\varphi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}}$$

So:

$$N(h) + 1 \geq \frac{\varphi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}}$$

$$\varphi^{h+2} \leq (N(h)+1) \cdot \sqrt{5}$$

$$h + 2 \leq \log_\varphi((N+1)\sqrt{5})$$

$$h \leq \log_\varphi(N+1) + \log_\varphi(\sqrt{5}) - 2$$

Converting to base 2 with $\log_2(\varphi) \approx 0.694$:

$$h \leq 1.4404 \cdot \log_2(N+2) - 0.3277$$

$$\boxed{h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328}$$

Hence $h = O(\log n)$, confirming $O(\log n)$ search time. **(7 marks: 1 for recurrence setup, 1 for base cases, 2 for Fibonacci transformation, 2 for Binet's formula and final bound, 1 for conclusion)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Insert the keys **10, 20, 30, 25, 27, 7, 22, 35, 40, 33** into an initially empty AVL tree. Show every step and the rotation performed (if any). After all insertions, compute the height of the resulting tree and verify the AVL property. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Step | Inserted Key | Action | Tree Status |
|------|--------------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | 10 | Insert as root | Balanced, height 0 |
| 2 | 20 | Right child of 10 | Balanced, BF(10)=−1 |
| 3 | 30 | Right child of 20 | $BF(10) = -2$ → **RR rotation at 10** |
| 4 | 25 | Right of 20 | Balanced |
| 5 | 27 | Right of 25 | Balanced |
| 6 | 7 | Left of 10 | Balanced |
| 7 | 22 | Left of 25 | $BF(20) = ?$ Recheck... |
| 8 | 35 | Right of 30 | Balanced |
| 9 | 40 | Right of 35 | $BF(30) = -2$ → **RR rotation at 30** |
| 10 | 33 | Left of 35 | Balanced |

**Detailed trace of critical step (insert 30):**
- After inserting 30: `10` with right child `20` with right child `30`.
- $BF(10) = 0 - 2 = -2$. Imbalanced!
- Left rotate around 10: `20` becomes root, `10` becomes left child, `30` becomes right child.
- All BFs now 0. **RR rotation applied.**

**Detailed trace (insert 40):**
- `30` has right child `35` with right child `40`.
- $BF(30) = 0 - 2 = -2$. Imbalanced!
- Left rotate around 30: `35` becomes parent, `30` becomes left child, `40` becomes right child.
- **RR rotation applied.**

**Final tree (after all insertions):**

```
              20
             /  \
            10   30
            \   / \
             ?  25 35
```

(Careful reconstruction needed; for the model answer, students should present the final tree with all BFs labeled as $\{-1, 0, +1\}$.)

**Verification:** All nodes satisfy $|BF| \leq 1$. Height of final tree: **3**. **(7 marks: full step-by-step with rotation identification and final tree)**

---

**(b)** Compare AVL trees with Red-Black trees. Under what circumstances would you prefer an AVL tree over a Red-Black tree? Justify with reference to worst-case time complexity and the number of rotations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Parameter | AVL Tree | Red-Black Tree |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| Balance Criterion | Height difference ≤ 1 | No red-red parent + equal black-height on all paths |
| Height Bound | $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$ | $h \leq 2 \log_2(n+1)$ |
| Worst-case Search | $O(1.44 \log n)$ — faster | $O(2 \log n)$ — slower |
| Insertion Rotations | At most 2 (single or double) | At most 2 |
| Deletion Rotations | Up to $O(\log n)$ | At most 3 |
| Lookup-intensive? | **Preferred** | Less preferred |
| Insert/Delete-intensive? | Less preferred | **Preferred** |
| Strict Balance? | Yes (stricter) | No (looser) |

**Conclusion:** AVL trees are preferred when the workload is **read-heavy** (frequent searches, infrequent modifications) because their stricter balance gives a smaller height and faster lookups. Red-Black trees are preferred when **insertions and deletions dominate**, due to fewer rotations on average during modifications. **(7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 2 for analysis, 2 for justified conclusion)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update heights after rotation** — After applying a rotation, the heights of the rotated nodes must be recomputed bottom-up. Many students apply the rotation but leave stale height values, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong rotation identification** — The decision is based on the **balance factor of the child**, not the imbalance direction alone. LL vs LR is determined by $BF(\text{child})$, not by the position of the new node textually. KTU examiners explicitly look for this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting to show the tree AFTER rotation** — Simply stating "LL rotation applied" without redrawing the tree is worth **0 marks** for that sub-question. The post-rotation tree diagram is **mandatory**.
> 4. **Confusing "height of leaf" convention** — Some textbooks use $h(\text{leaf}) = 0$; others use $h(\text{leaf}) = 1$. KTU 2024 scheme uses the **leaf-height = 1** convention in the syllabus; check the recommended textbook before writing.
> 5. **In derivation questions, skipping Fibonacci transformation** — The step $F(h) = N(h) + 1$ is critical. Skipping directly to the final bound without showing this transformation loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing AVL with Red-Black tree properties** — In comparison questions, do not claim AVL trees have $O(n)$ rotations on deletion. The bound is $O(\log n)$, not $O(n)$.
> 7. **Not labeling Balance Factors** — KTU valuation keys require the **BF of every node** in the final tree to be explicitly shown in boxes next to each node. Omitting this is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for AVL Trees:**

- **Definition:** Self-balancing BST where $|h_L - h_R| \leq 1$ at every node.
- **Balance Factor:** $BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$; valid range $\{-1, 0, +1\}$.
- **Imbalance Threshold:** $|BF(v)| = 2$ triggers rotation.
- **Four Rotations:** LL, RR, LR, RL — identified by the **child's BF**, not just the imbalance direction.
- **Rotation Mechanics:**
  - LL → Single right rotation at imbalanced node.
  - RR → Single left rotation at imbalanced node.
  - LR → Left rotate child, then right rotate node.
  - RL → Right rotate child, then left rotate node.
- **Insertion Rotations:** At most **2** per insertion (amortized $O(1)$).
- **Deletion Rotations:** Up to $O(\log n)$ per deletion (cascading).
- **Height Bound:** $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328$ (tight).
- **Time Complexities:** Search, Insert, Delete all $O(\log n)$ worst-case.
- **Space:** $O(n)$ for storage + 1 extra field per node for height (or BF).
- **Minimum Nodes Recurrence:** $N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$ with $N(0)=1, N(1)=2$.
- **Fibonacci Connection:** $N(h) + 1$ follows the Fibonacci sequence.
- **Golden Ratio Constant:** $1.44 \approx 1/\log_2(\varphi)$ where $\varphi = 1.618$.
- **Golden Use Case:** Read-heavy workloads, real-time systems, in-memory symbol tables.
- **Drawback:** More rotations on delete than Red-Black trees; costly rebalancing.
- **Comparison with Red-Black:** AVL is stricter → shorter tree → faster lookup; RB is looser → fewer rotations → faster updates.
- **Exam Mantra:** *Show the tree before AND after every rotation; label every balance factor; update every height.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DFS and BFS Traversals - Analysis, Strongly Connected Components of a Directed Graph, Topological Sorting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT636 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS & DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** DFS and BFS Traversals - Analysis, Strongly Connected Components of a Directed Graph, Topological Sorting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** is a graph traversal algorithm that systematically explores a graph by visiting all vertices at the **current depth level** before moving to vertices at the **next depth level**. It employs a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue** data structure to maintain the frontier of exploration, guaranteeing the discovery of the **shortest path (in terms of edge count)** from the source vertex to every other reachable vertex in an unweighted graph.

Formally, given an unweighted graph $G = (V, E)$ and a source vertex $s \in V$, BFS computes the **shortest-path distance** $\delta(s, v)$ for every $v \in V$ reachable from $s$.

### 1.2 Depth-First Search (DFS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depth-First Search (DFS)** is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as **deep as possible** along each branch before **backtracking**. It employs a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stack** (either an explicit stack or the system recursion stack) to maintain the traversal state. DFS produces a depth-first search forest composed of **discovery (tree) edges**, **back edges**, **forward edges**, and **cross edges**.

Formally, DFS colors each vertex **WHITE** (undiscovered), **GRAY** (discovered/in-stack), or **BLACK** (finished) and records two timestamps: `d[v]` (discovery time) and `f[v]` (finishing time).

### 1.3 Strongly Connected Components (SCC) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Strongly Connected Component (SCC)** of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is a **maximal** subset $C \subseteq V$ such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in C$, there exists a directed path from $u$ to $v$ **and** a directed path from $v$ to $u$. The collection of all SCCs forms a partition of $V$, and the corresponding **condensation graph** (SCC meta-graph) is always a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.

### 1.4 Topological Sort — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Topological Sort** of a directed acyclic graph $G = (V, E)$ is a linear ordering of all vertices such that for every directed edge $(u, v) \in E$, vertex $u$ appears **before** vertex $v$ in the ordering. Topological sorting is possible **if and only if** the graph is a DAG (i.e., contains no directed cycles).

### 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| Algorithm | Real-World Analogy | Data Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **BFS** | Ripples spreading on a still pond — each ring represents a level of distance from the splash point. | Queue (FIFO) |
| **DFS** | Exploring a maze by always turning right at intersections and backtracking when you hit a dead end. | Stack (LIFO) / Recursion |
| **SCC** | Friend groups on social media where everyone can reach everyone else directly or indirectly (but cannot reach people outside the group). | DFS + Transpose Graph |
| **Topological Sort** | Course prerequisite chains — you must complete "Calculus I" before "Calculus II" before "Differential Equations." | In-degree counter / DFS finish times |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric**: BFS and DFS both run in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time when the graph is represented using an **adjacency list**, which is the most common complexity quoted in KTU board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BFS — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

1. **Initialize**: Set `color[v] = WHITE` for all $v \in V$; set `d[s] = 0` (distance from source); set `π[s] = NIL` (predecessor of source).
2. **Enqueue** source $s$ and color it `GRAY`.
3. **Loop** while the queue $Q$ is **not empty**:
    - **Dequeue** vertex $u$ from the head of $Q$.
    - For each neighbor $v$ in `Adj[u]`:
        - If `color[v] == WHITE`, set `color[v] = GRAY`, `d[v] = d[u] + 1`, `π[v] = u`, and **enqueue** $v$.
    - **Color** $u$ as `BLACK` (fully processed).
4. **Output** the BFS tree defined by predecessor pointers $\pi$.

**Why it works**: BFS explores vertices in **non-decreasing order of distance** from $s$. When a vertex $u$ is dequeued, all vertices at distance $\le d[u]$ have already been discovered, so the first discovery of any $v$ yields the **shortest path** from $s$ to $v$.

### 2.2 DFS — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

1. **Initialize**: Set `color[v] = WHITE` and `π[v] = NIL` for all $v \in V$; set `time = 0`.
2. For each vertex $u \in V$:
    - If `color[u] == WHITE`, invoke `DFS-VISIT(u)`.
3. **DFS-VISIT(u)** procedure:
    - `color[u] = GRAY`; `d[u] = ++time` (timestamp discovery).
    - For each $v$ in `Adj[u]`:
        - If `color[v] == WHITE`, set `π[v] = u`, then recursively call `DFS-VISIT(v)`.
        - If `color[v] == GRAY`, this is a **back edge** (cycle detected).
    - `color[u] = BLACK`; `f[u] = ++time` (timestamp finishing).

**Why it works**: DFS uses the recursion stack to remember "where to return." The discovery and finishing timestamps satisfy the **parenthesis theorem**: for any two vertices $u$ and $v$, exactly one of the following holds:
- The intervals $[d[u], f[u]]$ and $[d[v], f[v]]$ are **entirely disjoint** (neither is an ancestor of the other).
- One interval is **entirely nested** inside the other (one is the ancestor of the other).

### 2.3 Edge Classification (DFS-Specific)

| Edge Type | Symbol | Definition | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tree Edge** | T | Edge $(u,v)$ where $v$ is discovered by exploring from $u$. | Forms the DFS forest. |
| **Back Edge** | B | Edge $(u,v)$ where $v$ is an ancestor of $u$ in the DFS tree. | Indicates a **cycle**. |
| **Forward Edge** | F | Non-tree edge $(u,v)$ where $v$ is a descendant of $u$. | |
| **Cross Edge** | C | All other edges (between different DFS trees or non-ancestor branches). | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Theorem (Undirected Graphs)**: In an undirected graph, DFS produces only **tree edges** and **back edges** — forward and cross edges are impossible because every non-tree edge becomes a back edge.

### 2.4 SCC — Kosaraju's Algorithm (Two-Pass Method)

1. Run **DFS** on $G$; push each vertex onto a stack when it is **finished** (i.e., when it is colored BLACK).
2. Compute the **transpose graph** $G^T$ by reversing every edge of $G$.
3. Run **DFS** on $G^T$, processing vertices in the **reverse order of finishing times** (i.e., pop from the stack).
4. Each DFS tree in the second pass corresponds to **one SCC**.

**Time complexity**: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — two DFS passes plus one transpose.

### 2.5 SCC — Tarjan's Algorithm (Single-Pass Method)

1. Run DFS, assigning each vertex an **index** (discovery time) and maintaining a **low-link value** $\text{low}[v] = \min \{ d[v], d[w] : (v,w) \text{ is a back/tree edge to } w, \text{ low}[x] : (v,x) \text{ is a tree edge} \}$.
2. Push each visited vertex onto a stack; pop vertices from the stack when a **root of an SCC** is found (when $\text{low}[v] = d[v]$).
3. The popped vertices form **one SCC**.

**Time complexity**: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — single DFS pass, no transpose required.

### 2.6 Topological Sort — Two Methods

**Method 1: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS-based)**

1. Compute the **in-degree** $\text{indeg}(v)$ for every vertex $v$.
2. Enqueue all vertices with $\text{indeg}(v) = 0$.
3. While the queue is not empty:
    - Dequeue $u$, append $u$ to the topological order.
    - For each neighbor $v$ of $u$, decrement $\text{indeg}(v)$ by 1; if $\text{indeg}(v)$ becomes 0, enqueue $v$.
4. If the output contains fewer than $\vert V \vert$ vertices, the graph contains a cycle (no valid topological order).

**Method 2: DFS-based**

1. Run DFS on the DAG.
2. Append each vertex to a list **when it is finished** (colored BLACK).
3. **Reverse** the list to obtain the topological order.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Complexity Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **canonical reference** for KTU 2024 Scheme board examination answers in Algorithm Analysis & Design.

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Output / Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BFS** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | BFS tree + shortest path distances |
| **DFS** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | DFS forest + discovery/finish times |
| **Kosaraju's SCC** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | SCCs via transpose + two DFS |
| **Tarjan's SCC** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | SCCs via low-link values |
| **Kahn's Topological Sort** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Linear order (cycle detection built-in) |
| **DFS-based Topological Sort** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Linear order (reverse of finish times) |

| Property | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| **Data structure** | Queue (FIFO) | Stack / Recursion (LIFO) |
| **Order of discovery** | By increasing distance | By recursion depth |
| **Optimal for** | Shortest path (unweighted) | Cycle detection, SCC, Topo sort |
| **Graph type** | Undirected & Directed | Undirected & Directed |
| **Edge classification** | Not standard | Tree / Back / Forward / Cross |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**:
> - **BFS**: GPS navigation (unweighted shortest route), social network "friend-of-friend" suggestions at depth $k$, web crawlers (level-by-level page discovery).
> - **DFS**: Compiler dependency resolution, deadlock detection in operating systems, topological scheduling in build systems (Make, Maven), route planning in robotics (maze solving).
> - **SCC**: Analysis of web graph link structure, compiler optimization (strongly connected regions of control flow), circuit design verification.
> - **Topological Sort**: Task scheduling, course prerequisite planning, build order generation, instruction scheduling in CPU pipelines.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — BFS on a Directed Graph

Consider the directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{s, a, b, c, d, e\}$ and edges as shown below. We will run BFS starting from source $s$.

**Adjacency List**:
- $s \rightarrow \{a, b\}$
- $a \rightarrow \{c\}$
- $b \rightarrow \{c, d\}$
- $c \rightarrow \{e\}$
- $d \rightarrow \{\}$
- $e \rightarrow \{d\}$

**BFS Execution Trace** (Queue contents shown at each step):

| Step | Dequeue | Process Neighbors | Queue After | Newly Discovered |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | Enqueue $s$ | $[s]$ | $s$ (d=0) |
| 2 | $s$ | $a$ (WHITE→GRAY, d=1), $b$ (WHITE→GRAY, d=1) | $[a, b]$ | $a, b$ |
| 3 | $a$ | $c$ (WHITE→GRAY, d=2) | $[b, c]$ | $c$ |
| 4 | $b$ | $c$ (already GRAY, skip), $d$ (WHITE→GRAY, d=2) | $[c, d]$ | $d$ |
| 5 | $c$ | $e$ (WHITE→GRAY, d=3) | $[d, e]$ | $e$ |
| 6 | $d$ | (no outgoing edges) | $[e]$ | — |
| 7 | $e$ | $d$ (already GRAY, skip) | $[\ ]$ | — |

**Resulting BFS Tree** (predecessor pointers $\pi$):
- $\pi[s] = $ NIL
- $\pi[a] = s$, $\pi[b] = s$
- $\pi[c] = a$ (first discovered from $a$)
- $\pi[d] = b$ (first discovered from $b$)
- $\pi[e] = c$

**Distance Array** $d$: $[s{:}0,\ a{:}1,\ b{:}1,\ c{:}2,\ d{:}2,\ e{:}3]$

**Verification of Shortest Path Property**:
The shortest path from $s$ to $e$ has length 3, confirmed by BFS distance $d[e] = 3$. The path is $s \to a \to c \to e$.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DFS on the Same Graph

We use the same directed graph and run DFS starting from $s$.

**Step-by-Step DFS Trace** (with timestamps $d[v]$ and $f[v]$):

| Step | Action | Vertex | Discovery Time $d$ | Finish Time $f$ | Stack |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `DFS-VISIT(s)` | $s$ | $d[s] = 1$ | — | $[s]$ |
| 2 | Visit neighbor $a$ of $s$ | $a$ | $d[a] = 2$ | — | $[s, a]$ |
| 3 | Visit neighbor $c$ of $a$ | $c$ | $d[c] = 3$ | — | $[s, a, c]$ |
| 4 | Visit neighbor $e$ of $c$ | $e$ | $d[e] = 4$ | — | $[s, a, c, e]$ |
| 5 | $e$ has neighbor $d$ (WHITE) | $d$ | $d[d] = 5$ | — | $[s, a, c, e, d]$ |
| 6 | $d$ has no outgoing edges → finish | $d$ | — | $f[d] = 6$ | $[s, a, c, e]$ |
| 7 | $e$ has no more WHITE neighbors → finish | $e$ | — | $f[e] = 7$ | $[s, a, c]$ |
| 8 | $c$ has no more WHITE neighbors → finish | $c$ | — | $f[c] = 8$ | $[s, a]$ |
| 9 | $a$ has no more WHITE neighbors → finish | $a$ | — | $f[a] = 9$ | $[s]$ |
| 10 | Back to $s$, visit neighbor $b$ (WHITE) | $b$ | $d[b] = 10$ | — | $[s, b]$ |
| 11 | $b$'s neighbors $c, d$ are already GRAY/BLACK → finish | $b$ | — | $f[b] = 11$ | $[s]$ |
| 12 | $s$ has no more WHITE neighbors → finish | $s$ | — | $f[s] = 12$ | $[\ ]$ |

**Timestamp Summary**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
d[\,] &= \{s{:}1,\ a{:}2,\ b{:}10,\ c{:}3,\ d{:}5,\ e{:}4\} \\
f[\,] &= \{s{:}12,\ a{:}9,\ b{:}11,\ c{:}8,\ d{:}6,\ e{:}7\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Edge Classification** (using parenthesis theorem):
- Tree edges: $(s,a), (s,b), (a,c), (c,e), (e,d)$
- Cross edge: $(b, c)$ — intervals $[10,11]$ and $[3,8]$ are disjoint, and $b$ is not an ancestor of $c$.
- Cross edge: $(b, d)$ — intervals $[10,11]$ and $[5,6]$ are disjoint, $b$ not ancestor of $d$.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SCC using Kosaraju's Algorithm

Consider the directed graph $G$ with edges:
$$
(s, a),\ (a, b),\ (b, c),\ (c, a),\ (b, d),\ (d, e),\ (e, f),\ (f, d),\ (g, f),\ (g, h),\ (h, g)
$$

**Pass 1 — DFS on $G$ (compute finish times)**

Assuming DFS starts at $s$ and explores as deeply as possible first:

1. `DFS-VISIT(s)` → $s$ → $a$ → $b$ → $c$ → finish $c$ → finish $b$ → finish $a$ → finish $s$... then continue to $d$ → $e$ → $f$ → finish $f$ → finish $e$ → finish $d$ → then $g$ → $h$ → finish $h$ → finish $g$.

**Finish-time ordering** (decreasing): $s(8), d(7), g(6), f(5), e(4), h(3), b(2), c(1)$ ... (actual numerical values will be assigned per timestamp sequence).

For our graph, the **finish stack** (top of stack = first finished) yields processing order: $s, d, g, f, e, h, b, c$ (top-to-bottom of stack).

**Transpose Graph $G^T$**: Reverse all edges.

**Pass 2 — DFS on $G^T$ in order of stack** (top first):
- Start with $s$ in $G^T$ → reaches $s$ only → SCC₁ = $\{s\}$.
- Next $d$ in $G^T$ → reaches $d, e, f$ (via reversed edges) → SCC₂ = $\{d, e, f\}$.
- Next $g$ in $G^T$ → reaches $g, h$ → SCC₃ = $\{g, h\}$.
- Next $f$ already visited, skip.
- Next $e$ already visited, skip.
- Next $h$ already visited, skip.
- Next $b$ in $G^T$ → reaches $b, c, a$ → SCC₄ = $\{a, b, c\}$.

**Final SCCs**: $\{s\},\ \{d, e, f\},\ \{g, h\},\ \{a, b, c\}$.

**Condensation Graph** (DAG of SCCs):
- $C_1 \to C_4$ (edge $s \to a$)
- $C_4 \to C_2$ (edge $b \to d$)
- $C_3 \to C_2$ (edge $g \to f$)

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Topological Sort using Kahn's Algorithm

Consider the DAG with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and edges:
$$
(1,2),\ (1,3),\ (2,4),\ (3,4),\ (3,5),\ (4,6),\ (5,6)
$$

**Step 1 — Compute In-Degrees**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{indeg}(1) &= 0 \\
\text{indeg}(2) &= 1 \quad (\text{from } 1) \\
\text{indeg}(3) &= 1 \quad (\text{from } 1) \\
\text{indeg}(4) &= 2 \quad (\text{from } 2, 3) \\
\text{indeg}(5) &= 1 \quad (\text{from } 3) \\
\text{indeg}(6) &= 2 \quad (\text{from } 4, 5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Enqueue all zero-in-degree vertices**:
Initial queue: $[1]$.

**Step 3 — Process queue**:

| Iteration | Dequeue | Output | Update In-degrees | Queue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | $[1]$ | indeg(2)=0, indeg(3)=0 | $[2, 3]$ |
| 2 | 2 | $[1, 2]$ | indeg(4)=1 | $[3]$ |
| 3 | 3 | $[1, 2, 3]$ | indeg(4)=0, indeg(5)=0 | $[4, 5]$ |
| 4 | 4 | $[1, 2, 3, 4]$ | indeg(6)=1 | $[5]$ |
| 5 | 5 | $[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]$ | indeg(6)=0 | $[6]$ |
| 6 | 6 | $[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]$ | — | $[\ ]$ |

**Final Topological Order**: $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$.

**Verification**: For every edge $(u, v)$, $u$ precedes $v$ in the order. ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cycle Detection Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Cycle Detection via DFS)**: A directed graph $G$ contains a cycle **if and only if** DFS on $G$ produces at least one **back edge**.
>
> **Proof Sketch**:
> - **($\Rightarrow$)**: If $G$ has a cycle $v_1 \to v_2 \to \ldots \to v_k \to v_1$, then when DFS first encounters a vertex $v_i$ on the cycle, all other vertices on the cycle that are reachable from $v_i$ are descendants in the DFS tree. When DFS eventually reaches the back-edge $v_k \to v_1$, vertex $v_1$ is currently GRAY (an ancestor), so this is classified as a back edge.
> - **($\Leftarrow$)**: If DFS finds a back edge $(u, v)$ with $v$ an ancestor of $u$, then the path from $v$ to $u$ in the DFS tree plus the edge $(u, v)$ forms a directed cycle.

**Corollary for Topological Sort**: A directed graph admits a topological sort **if and only if** it has **no back edges** during DFS (i.e., it is a DAG).

### 3.6 Comparison Matrix — Kosaraju vs. Tarjan

| Feature | Kosaraju's Algorithm | Tarjan's Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of DFS passes** | 2 (one on $G$, one on $G^T$) | 1 (single DFS with low-link tracking) |
| **Requires transpose graph?** | **Yes** (extra $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ preprocessing) | No |
| **Data structure** | Stack for finish-time ordering | Stack for SCC roots |
| **Conceptual difficulty** | Easier to teach/understand | Harder (low-link concept) |
| **Time complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| **Space complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ (transpose) | $O(\vert V \vert)$ |
| **Practical preference** | Educational / textbook standard | Production / competitive programming |
