## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BFS Traversal — Queue-Based State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start BFS from source s]) --> initA[Color all WHITE, d s = 0]
    initA --> enqA[Enqueue s, color s = GRAY]
    enqA --> checkQ{Queue empty?}
    checkQ -- Yes --> endA([End BFS])
    checkQ -- No --> deqA[Dequeue vertex u]
    deqA --> scanA[Scan all neighbors v of u]
    scanA --> condA{color v = WHITE?}
    condA -- No --> scanA
    condA -- Yes --> setA["d v = d u + 1, pi v = u, color v = GRAY, enqueue v"]
    setA --> scanA
    scanA --> doneA[All neighbors processed]
    doneA --> blackA[color u = BLACK]
    blackA --> checkQ
```

### 4.2 DFS Traversal — Recursion Stack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start DFS]) --> pickB[Pick a WHITE vertex u]
    pickB --> visitB["color u = GRAY, d u = time++"]
    visitB --> scanB[Iterate neighbors v of u]
    scanB --> condB{color v = WHITE?}
    condB -- Yes --> recB[pi v = u, recursive DFS-VISIT v]
    recB --> scanB
    condB -- No --> kindB{color v = GRAY?}
    kindB -- Yes --> backE["Back Edge - cycle detected"]
    kindB -- No --> crossE["Forward or Cross Edge"]
    backE --> scanB
    crossE --> scanB
    scanB --> moreB{More WHITE vertices in V?}
    moreB -- Yes --> pickB
    moreB -- No --> endB([End DFS])
```

### 4.3 Strongly Connected Components — Conceptual View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph originalG["Original Directed Graph G"]
        n1[Vertex a]
        n2[Vertex b]
        n3[Vertex c]
        n4[Vertex d]
        n5[Vertex e]
        n1 <--> n2
        n2 --> n3
        n3 --> n1
        n2 --> n4
        n4 --> n5
    end
    subgraph condG["Condensation Graph (DAG of SCCs)"]
        scc1["SCC1 = a, b, c"]
        scc2["SCC2 = d"]
        scc3["SCC3 = e"]
    end
    scc1 --> scc2
    scc2 --> scc3
```

### 4.4 Topological Sort — Kahn's Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startK([Start]) --> indegK[Compute in-degree of every vertex]
    indegK --> initQK[Enqueue all vertices with in-degree = 0]
    initQK --> emptyK{Queue empty?}
    emptyK -- Yes --> chkK{Output count = V?}
    chkK -- Yes --> doneK([Valid topological order])
    chkK -- No --> cycleK([Cycle detected - no valid order])
    emptyK -- No --> popK[Dequeue vertex u, append to order]
    popK --> updK[For each neighbor v of u: indeg v minus 1]
    updK --> zeroK{indeg v = 0?}
    zeroK -- Yes --> enqK[Enqueue v]
    zeroK -- No --> updK
    enqK --> emptyK
    updK --> emptyK
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DFS as a Subroutine Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stage1["Stage 1 Input"]
        inputG["Directed Graph G = V, E"]
        sourceS["Source / Start Vertex s"]
    end
    subgraph stage2["Stage 2 Preprocessing"]
        colorInit["Color Array: all WHITE"]
        piInit["Predecessor Array: all NIL"]
        timeInit["Global time = 0"]
    end
    subgraph stage3["Stage 3 Core DFS Engine"]
        visit["DFS-VISIT u: stamp d u, recurse on WHITE neighbors"]
        stampF["On return: stamp f u, mark BLACK"]
        edgeClass["Edge Classifier: Tree / Back / Forward / Cross"]
    end
    subgraph stage4["Stage 4 Post-Processing"]
        sccOut["SCC Extraction via Kosaraju or Tarjan"]
        topoOut["Topological Order via reverse of finish times"]
    end
    inputG --> colorInit
    sourceS --> visit
    colorInit --> visit
    piInit --> visit
    timeInit --> visit
    visit --> stampF
    stampF --> edgeClass
    stampF --> sccOut
    stampF --> topoOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above illustrates how **DFS acts as a foundational subroutine** that powers multiple advanced algorithms (SCC detection, topological sort, cycle detection, articulation-point finding). In KTU exam answers, emphasizing this **reusability** of DFS earns full methodology marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between BFS and DFS. Mention the data structure used and one key application of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> | Aspect | BFS | DFS |
> |---|---|---|
> | Data Structure | Queue (FIFO) | Stack / Recursion (LIFO) |
> | Traversal Pattern | Level-by-level (breadth-wise) | Branch-by-branch (depth-wise) |
> | Key Application | Shortest path in unweighted graph | Topological sorting, cycle detection |
> | Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
> *(1 mark for data structure, 1 mark for traversal pattern, 1 mark for application)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** Define a Strongly Connected Component. State the time complexity of Kosaraju's algorithm.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A Strongly Connected Component (SCC) of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is a **maximal subset** $C \subseteq V$ such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in C$, both $u \rightsquigarrow v$ and $v \rightsquigarrow u$ exist as directed paths. *(2 marks)*
> The time complexity of Kosaraju's algorithm is $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. *(1 mark)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B-A]**
> **(a)** Explain Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm in detail. Apply BFS on the following directed graph starting from vertex **1**, and construct the BFS tree. Show the queue state at each step. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Edges**: $(1,2),\ (1,3),\ (2,4),\ (3,4),\ (3,5),\ (4,6),\ (5,6)$
>
> **(b)** Explain Depth-First Search (DFS) algorithm. For the same graph, perform DFS starting from vertex **1** and list the discovery and finishing times. Classify each edge. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**BFS Algorithm** *(2 marks for explanation)*

1. Initialize `color[v] = WHITE` for all $v \in V$.
2. Set `d[s] = 0`, `π[s] = NIL`; color $s$ GRAY and enqueue it.
3. While $Q$ is not empty:
    - Dequeue $u$; for each $v \in \text{Adj}[u]$:
        - If `color[v] = WHITE`: set `d[v] = d[u] + 1`, `π[v] = u`, color $v$ GRAY, enqueue $v$.
    - Color $u$ BLACK.

**Adjacency List**:
- 1 → {2, 3}, 2 → {4}, 3 → {4, 5}, 4 → {6}, 5 → {6}, 6 → {}

**Execution Table** *(5 marks)*:

| Step | Dequeue | Process | Queue After |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | Enqueue 1 | [1] |
| 2 | 1 | Discover 2 (d=1), 3 (d=1) | [2, 3] |
| 3 | 2 | Discover 4 (d=2) | [3, 4] |
| 4 | 3 | 4 already GRAY; discover 5 (d=2) | [4, 5] |
| 5 | 4 | Discover 6 (d=3) | [5, 6] |
| 6 | 5 | 6 already GRAY | [6] |
| 7 | 6 | No outgoing | [ ] |

**BFS Tree** (predecessors): $\pi[2]=1, \pi[3]=1, \pi[4]=2, \pi[5]=3, \pi[6]=4$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**DFS Algorithm** *(2 marks for explanation)*

1. Initialize all vertices WHITE, set `time = 0`.
2. For each WHITE vertex $u$, call `DFS-VISIT(u)`:
    - `color[u] = GRAY`, `d[u] = ++time`.
    - For each $v \in \text{Adj}[u]$:
        - If WHITE: set `π[v] = u`, recursively visit.
        - If GRAY: back edge (cycle).
    - `color[u] = BLACK`, `f[u] = ++time`.

**Execution Trace** *(5 marks)*:

| Action | Vertex | d[v] | f[v] |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visit 1 | 1 | 1 | — |
| → 2 | 2 | 2 | — |
| → 4 | 4 | 3 | — |
| → 6 | 6 | 4 | 5 |
| Back to 4 | 4 | — | 6 |
| Back to 2 | 2 | — | 7 |
| Back to 1, → 3 | 3 | 8 | — |
| 4 already BLACK, → 5 | 5 | 9 | — |
| 6 already BLACK | 5 | — | 10 |
| Back to 3 | 3 | — | 11 |
| Back to 1 | 1 | — | 12 |

**Timestamp Summary**:
- $d = \{1{:}1, 2{:}2, 3{:}8, 4{:}3, 5{:}9, 6{:}4\}$
- $f = \{1{:}12, 2{:}7, 3{:}11, 4{:}6, 5{:}10, 6{:}5\}$

**Edge Classification**:
- **Tree edges**: $(1,2), (1,3), (2,4), (4,6), (3,5)$
- **Cross edges**: $(3,4)$ — intervals $[8,11]$ and $[3,6]$ are disjoint
- **Cross edge**: $(5,6)$ — intervals $[9,10]$ and $[4,5]$ are disjoint

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [INTERNAL CHOICE]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B-B]**
> **(a)** Explain Kosaraju's algorithm to find Strongly Connected Components (SCCs) of a directed graph. Apply it on the given graph and list all SCCs. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Edges of the graph**:
> $(1,2),\ (2,3),\ (3,1),\ (3,4),\ (4,5),\ (5,3),\ (6,5),\ (6,7),\ (7,8),\ (8,6)$
>
> **(b)** Explain Topological Sorting. Apply Kahn's algorithm on the following DAG and produce the topological order. Show the in-degree updates at each step. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **DAG Edges**: $(A,B),\ (A,C),\ (B,D),\ (C,D),\ (C,E),\ (D,F),\ (E,F)$

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Kosaraju's Algorithm** *(3 marks for explanation)*

1. Run DFS on $G$. When a vertex finishes, push it onto a stack.
2. Compute transpose $G^T$ (reverse all edges).
3. Pop vertices from the stack one by one; run DFS on $G^T$ starting from each popped vertex. Each DFS tree = one SCC.

**Pass 1 — DFS on G** *(2 marks)*:

| Vertex | d[v] | f[v] |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 8 |
| 2 | 2 | 7 |
| 3 | 3 | 6 |
| 4 | 4 | 5 |
| 5 | 9 | 10 (after DFS from 1-2-3-4-5 finishes, restart at 6) |
| 6 | 11 | 16 |
| 7 | 12 | 15 |
| 8 | 13 | 14 |

**Stack (top to bottom)**: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 (8 is popped first as it finishes last — wait, **finished first** is pushed first; **finished last** is on top).

**Stack top → bottom order of popping**: 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 7, 6 *(reverse finish times; 6 finishes last = popped first)*

**Pass 2 — DFS on $G^T$** *(2 marks)*:
- Start with **6** in $G^T$: reaches 6 → 8 → 7 → 6 (cycle), and 6 → 5 (via reverse of $(5,6)$) → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1. So one giant SCC? Let's check carefully.

**Transpose Edges**: $(2,1),\ (3,2),\ (1,3),\ (4,3),\ (5,4),\ (3,5),\ (5,6),\ (7,6),\ (8,7),\ (6,8)$

- Start at **6**: $6 \to 8 \to 7$ (dead end at 7). Stack: $\{6, 8, 7\}$.
- From 6, also go to **5**: $5 \to 4 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1$. All visited.
- **SCC₁ = $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$** (all 8 vertices).

**Final SCCs**: One single SCC containing all 8 vertices. The graph is strongly connected.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Topological Sort Definition** *(2 marks for explanation)*

A linear ordering of vertices of a DAG such that for every directed edge $(u, v)$, $u$ appears before $v$ in the ordering.

**Kahn's Algorithm Steps**:
1. Compute in-degree of every vertex.
2. Enqueue all zero-in-degree vertices.
3. Dequeue, output, decrement in-degrees of neighbors; enqueue any that become zero.
4. Repeat until queue is empty.

**Initial In-Degrees** *(1 mark)*:
- $\text{indeg}(A) = 0$, $\text{indeg}(B) = 1$, $\text{indeg}(C) = 1$, $\text{indeg}(D) = 2$, $\text{indeg}(E) = 1$, $\text{indeg}(F) = 2$

**Execution Table** *(4 marks)*:

| Step | Dequeue | Output | In-degree updates | Queue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | [A] | indeg(B)=0, indeg(C)=0 | [B, C] |
| 2 | B | [A, B] | indeg(D)=1 | [C] |
| 3 | C | [A, B, C] | indeg(D)=0, indeg(E)=0 | [D, E] |
| 4 | D | [A, B, C, D] | indeg(F)=1 | [E] |
| 5 | E | [A, B, C, D, E] | indeg(F)=0 | [F] |
| 6 | F | [A, B, C, D, E, F] | — | [ ] |

**Final Topological Order**: $A, B, C, D, E, F$ (or any valid equivalent ordering depending on tie-breaking).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong edge classification (DFS)**: Forgetting that in a **directed** graph, DFS can produce **cross and forward edges**, but in an **undirected** graph, only **tree and back edges** exist. Many students incorrectly classify cross edges as back edges in directed graphs.
> 2. **Topological sort on a non-DAG**: If the input graph contains a cycle, Kahn's algorithm will leave some vertices unprocessed. Students often **force** an ordering without checking — always verify that **output count = |V|** before declaring success.
> 3. **SCC — forgetting to reverse the order**: In Kosaraju's algorithm, vertices must be processed in **decreasing order of finish times**. A common mistake is to process in the order they were finished (which gives the *opposite* SCCs or merged SCCs).
> 4. **BFS predecessor vs. distance confusion**: The BFS tree is defined by `π[v]`, not by `d[v]`. Drawing the tree using distances instead of predecessor edges will lose marks.
> 5. **Omitting queue state in the answer**: KTU board examiners **require** the queue state to be shown at every step in BFS. Skipping this loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Time complexity justification**: Simply writing $O(V + E)$ is not enough — KTU 2024 expects you to **justify** that this is $\Theta(\vert V \vert)$ for initialization plus $O(\vert E \vert)$ for scanning adjacency lists (since each edge is examined exactly twice in undirected, once in directed).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — KTU 2024 Module 2**

- ✅ **BFS uses a QUEUE (FIFO)**, **DFS uses a STACK / RECURSION (LIFO)**. This is the single most tested distinction.
- ✅ Both BFS and DFS run in **$O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$** time on an **adjacency-list** representation; both use **$O(\vert V \vert)$** auxiliary space.
- ✅ **BFS** is optimal for **shortest path in unweighted graphs**; **DFS** is the building block for **cycle detection, topological sort, SCC, and articulation-point algorithms**.
- ✅ **DFS edge types** in a directed graph: **Tree, Back, Forward, Cross**; in an undirected graph: only **Tree and Back**.
- ✅ **Cycle in directed graph $\iff$ DFS finds a back edge** (a GRAY-to-GRAY edge).
- ✅ **SCC** is a **maximal** mutually-reachable subset; **every vertex belongs to exactly one SCC**.
- ✅ **Kosaraju's algorithm** = two DFS passes + transpose; **Tarjan's** = one DFS with low-link values.
- ✅ **Condensation graph of SCCs is always a DAG**.
- ✅ **Topological sort exists $\iff$ graph is a DAG**. Two methods: **Kahn's (BFS-based, uses in-degrees)** and **DFS-based (reverse of finish times)**.
- ✅ In **Kahn's algorithm**, if output count $< \vert V \vert$ at the end, the graph contains a **cycle** — this is the standard KTU cycle-detection question.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems, always **draw the graph, list the adjacency matrix/list, and tabulate the queue/stack state at every step** — this guarantees full marks.
- ✅ Standard complexity symbols: $O(\cdot)$ for upper bound, $\Theta(\cdot)$ for tight bound, $\Omega(\cdot)$ for lower bound. BFS/DFS in the standard model are $\Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- ✅ The **parenthesis theorem** states: intervals $[d[u], f[u]]$ and $[d[v], f[v]]$ are either disjoint or nested — never partially overlapping. This is the basis of edge classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Control Abstraction of Divide and Conquer - 2-Way Merge Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT636 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS & DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Control Abstraction of Divide and Conquer - 2-Way Merge Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: The Control Abstraction of Divide and Conquer

In the context of **Algorithm Analysis and Design**, the **Control Abstraction of Divide and Conquer (D&C)** is a generic, high-level procedural template that captures the unified recursive structure shared by all algorithms built on the D&C paradigm. It encapsulates three sequential phases — **Divide**, **Conquer**, and **Combine** — into a single reusable framework, allowing algorithm designers to focus only on the problem-specific logic while the recursion engine handles the structural flow.

The abstract procedure is formally expressed in pseudocode as:

```
Algorithm DAndC(P)
1.  if Small(P) then return S(P)
2.  else
3.      Divide P into smaller sub-problems P1, P2, ..., Pk
4.      Apply DAndC to each sub-problem Pi
5.      Combine the solutions to P1, P2, ..., Pk to obtain the solution to P
6.  end if
```

Where:
- **P** represents the problem instance to be solved.
- **Small(P)** is a boolean predicate that checks whether the problem is small enough to be solved directly.
- **S(P)** is the direct (brute-force/base-case) solution.
- **k ≥ 2** is the number of sub-problems generated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The control abstraction is a *meta-algorithm*. It does not solve any specific problem; it merely orchestrates the flow. Specific instances (Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen's Multiplication) are concrete realizations of this template.

### 1.2 Formal Definition: 2-Way Merge Sort

**2-Way Merge Sort** is the canonical instantiation of the Divide and Conquer control abstraction for the sorting problem. It was invented by **John von Neumann (1945)**. The algorithm:
- **Divides** the unsorted array of size $n$ into two equal (or near-equal) sub-arrays of size $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ and $\lceil n/2 \rceil$.
- **Conquers** (recursively sorts) each sub-array independently.
- **Combines** the two sorted sub-arrays into a single sorted array using a linear-time **Merge** procedure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics for 2-Way Merge Sort**
> - **Time Complexity:** $\Theta(n \log_2 n)$ in **all three cases** (best, average, worst).
> - **Space Complexity:** $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary space.
> - **Stability:** **Stable** (equal keys retain their original relative order).
> - **In-Place:** **No** (requires an auxiliary buffer array).
> - **Recursion Type:** Top-down (natural variant is bottom-up iterative).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Tournament Bracket Analogy (Control Abstraction):**
Imagine organizing a knockout tennis tournament with 64 players. You don't make all 64 play at once. Instead:
1. **Divide:** Split them into 32 matches of 2 players.
2. **Conquer:** Each match produces one winner (the "small" problem — 2 players, easily decided).
3. **Combine:** The 32 winners form the next round of 16 matches, and so on.

The tournament committee doesn't need to know *how* each match is played (best of 3? best of 5?). They only enforce the *bracket structure*. The bracket structure is the **Control Abstraction**; the specific match rules are the **concrete algorithm** plugged into it.

**The Card-Sorting Analogy (Merge Sort):**
You have a shuffled deck of 52 cards spread face-up on a table. To sort them:
1. **Divide:** Split the deck into two piles of 26 cards each.
2. **Conquer:** Recursively split each pile until you have 52 piles of 1 card each (a pile of 1 is trivially sorted).
3. **Combine:** Repeatedly merge pairs of piles by exposing the top card of each pile and placing the smaller one on the output pile — like merging two sorted spools of thread.

The brilliance is that merging two **already sorted** lists is dramatically cheaper ($O(n)$) than sorting a random list ($O(n \log n)$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Phases of the Control Abstraction — A Structured Breakdown

| Phase | Logical Action | Purpose | D&C Generic Code Line |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Base Case Check** | `if Small(P) then S(P)` | Stop recursion when problem size becomes trivially solvable. Prevents infinite recursion. | Line 1 |
| **Phase 2: Divide** | Partition `P` into `k ≥ 2` sub-problems `P1, P2, ..., Pk` | Reduce problem complexity. Each `|Pi| < |P|`. | Line 3 |
| **Phase 3: Conquer (Recursive Call)** | Apply `DAndC` to each `Pi` | Solve sub-problems independently. | Line 4 |
| **Phase 4: Combine** | Merge results into the final answer | Synthesize the global solution from local solutions. | Line 5 |

### 2.2 Deep Dive: The `Merge` Procedure (Heart of 2-Way Merge Sort)

The `Merge(A, low, mid, high)` procedure is the **Combine** step. Given two sorted contiguous sub-arrays:
- `A[low ... mid]` (size $n_1$)
- `A[mid+1 ... high]` (size $n_2$)

It produces a single sorted array `A[low ... high]`.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. Allocate two auxiliary arrays `L ← A[low ... mid]` and `R ← A[mid+1 ... high]` of sizes $n_1 = mid - low + 1$ and $n_2 = high - mid$.
2. Place **sentinel values** $+\infty$ at the end of both `L` and `R` (this is the *Sentinel Card* trick from CLRS textbook, eliminating boundary checks).
3. Maintain three pointers: $i = 1$ (into $L$), $j = 1$ (into $R$), $k = low$ (into $A$).
4. **While** $i \leq n_1$ **and** $j \leq n_2$:
    - If $L[i] \leq R[j]$, set $A[k] \leftarrow L[i]$ and increment $i$.
    - Else, set $A[k] \leftarrow R[j]$ and increment $j$.
    - Increment $k$.
5. Copy any remaining elements of $L$ or $R$ back into $A$ (sentinels make this loop-free).

**Why the Sentinel Trick?**
Without sentinels, we would need an extra `while` loop to copy the leftover tail. With $+\infty$ sentinels, the comparison $L[i] \leq R[j]$ will always be true once one array is exhausted, automatically draining the remainder.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility of 2-Way Merge Sort:**
> - **External Sorting:** When datasets are too large to fit in RAM (databases, Hadoop), the *external merge sort* is the standard. It sorts chunks that fit in memory, then merges them on disk. Time complexity remains $O(n \log n)$ with minimal disk I/O.
> - **Linked List Sorting:** Merge sort is the preferred algorithm for singly linked lists because it requires no random access and achieves $O(n \log n)$ time with $O(1)$ extra space (using slow/fast pointer trick for finding the midpoint).
> - **Stable Sort Requirement:** Whenever stability matters (e.g., sorting students by grade, then by name as tiebreaker), merge sort's stability is invaluable.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Entity** | **Formula / Relation** | **Meaning / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Master Recurrence (2-Way Merge Sort) | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | 2 recursive calls on half-size, plus $\Theta(n)$ merge cost. |
| Solved Time Complexity | $T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$ | Holds for best, average, and worst cases. |
| Recursion Tree Levels | $\log_2 n + 1$ | Tree height (root at level 0). |
| Work Per Level | $\Theta(n)$ | Each level merges all $n$ elements exactly once. |
| Total Work | $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n} \Theta(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$ | Geometric sum of identical per-level costs. |
| Merge Cost | $C(n) = \Theta(n)$ | Each element is compared and moved at most once. |
| Divide Cost | $D(n) = \Theta(1)$ | One midpoint calculation: $mid = \lfloor (low + high)/2 \rfloor$. |
| Conquer Cost | $2 \cdot T(n/2)$ | Two independent recursive calls. |
| Auxiliary Space | $\Theta(n)$ | One buffer array of size $n$. |
| Stack Space (Recursion) | $\Theta(\log_2 n)$ | Maximum call-stack depth. |
| Number of Comparisons (Worst) | $n \log_2 n - n + 1$ | Exact bound from CLRS. |
| Number of Comparisons (Best) | $n \log_2 n / 2$ | When merging balanced equal-sized sub-arrays. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Master Recurrence for 2-Way Merge Sort

We will derive $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ rigorously from first principles and then solve it using the **Recursion Tree Method** (a KTU examiner favorite).

**Step 1 — Set up the recurrence from the algorithm structure.**

Let $T(n)$ be the worst-case running time of `MergeSort` on an input of size $n$.

The algorithm performs three actions:
1. **Divide:** Computes `mid = (low + high) / 2` → constant time $\Theta(1)$.
2. **Conquer:** Recursively sorts two sub-arrays of size $n/2$ → $2T(n/2)$.
3. **Combine:** Calls `Merge` on the full array of size $n$ → $\Theta(n)$ time.

Summing these contributions:

$$
T(n) = \underbrace{\Theta(1)}_{\text{Divide}} + \underbrace{2T(n/2)}_{\text{Conquer}} + \underbrace{\Theta(n)}_{\text{Combine}}
$$

Dropping the $\Theta(1)$ term as it is dominated for $n \geq 2$:

$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve via the Recursion Tree Method.**

Expand the recurrence as a tree. The merge cost $cn$ (for some constant $c > 0$) is distributed across levels.

**Level 0 (root):** One problem of size $n$.
- Sub-problems: 1
- Size of each: $n$
- Work at this level: $1 \cdot cn = cn$

**Level 1:** Two problems, each of size $n/2$.
- Sub-problems: 2
- Size of each: $n/2$
- Work at this level: $2 \cdot c(n/2) = cn$

**Level 2:** Four problems, each of size $n/4$.
- Sub-problems: 4
- Size of each: $n/4$
- Work at this level: $4 \cdot c(n/4) = cn$

**General Level $i$:** $2^i$ sub-problems, each of size $n/2^i$.
- Work at level $i$: $2^i \cdot c(n/2^i) = cn$.

**Step 3 — Determine the height of the tree.**

Recursion stops when sub-problem size $= 1$ (the base case for a single element requires constant work).

$$
\frac{n}{2^i} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2^i = n \quad \Rightarrow \quad i = \log_2 n
$$

So the tree has $L = \log_2 n + 1$ levels (levels $0$ through $\log_2 n$).

**Step 4 — Sum the work across all levels.**

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n} cn = cn \cdot (\log_2 n + 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply asymptotic notation.**

$$
T(n) = cn \log_2 n + cn = \Theta(n \log_2 n)
$$

Thus, the closed-form solution is:

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)}
$$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Tracing Merge Sort

**Input Array:** $A = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]$

We trace the algorithm by showing the **Divide** tree (top-down) and the **Combine/Merge** sequence (bottom-up).

**Divide Phase (Recursion tree, top-down):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10] &\rightarrow \text{mid} = 3 \\[2pt]
[38, 27, 43, 3] \quad | \quad [9, 82, 10] &\rightarrow \text{recurse on each} \\[2pt]
[38, 27] \quad | \quad [43, 3] \quad | \quad [9, 82] \quad | \quad [10] &\rightarrow \text{recurse} \\[2pt]
[38] \mid [27] \quad | \quad [43] \mid [3] \quad | \quad [9] \mid [82] \quad | \quad [10]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combine Phase (Merging bottom-up):**

| Merge Step | Left Sub-array | Right Sub-array | Merged Result | Comparisons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[38]$ | $[27]$ | $[27, 38]$ | 1 |
| 2 | $[43]$ | $[3]$ | $[3, 43]$ | 1 |
| 3 | $[9]$ | $[82]$ | $[9, 82]$ | 1 |
| 4 | $[9, 82]$ | $[10]$ | $[9, 10, 82]$ | 2 |
| 5 | $[27, 38]$ | $[3, 43]$ | $[3, 27, 38, 43]$ | 3 |
| 6 | $[3, 27, 38, 43]$ | $[9, 10, 82]$ | $[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]$ | 6 |

**Total Comparisons:** $1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 6 = 14$

**Verification with formula** (for $n = 7$): $n \log_2 n - n + 1 = 7 \cdot \log_2 7 - 7 + 1 \approx 7(2.807) - 6 \approx 13.65 \approx 14$ ✓

The sorted output is:

$$
A_{\text{sorted}} = [3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]
$$

### 3.3 Detailed Pseudocode (Standard CLRS-Style)

```
Algorithm MergeSort(A, low, high)
1.  if low < high then
2.      mid ← floor((low + high) / 2)
3.      MergeSort(A, low, mid)        // Conquer left half
4.      MergeSort(A, mid+1, high)     // Conquer right half
5.      Merge(A, low, mid, high)      // Combine
6.  end if

Algorithm Merge(A, low, mid, high)
1.  n1 ← mid - low + 1
2.  n2 ← high - mid
3.  Create arrays L[1..n1+1] and R[1..n2+1]
4.  for i ← 1 to n1 do L[i] ← A[low + i - 1]
5.  for j ← 1 to n2 do R[j] ← A[mid + j]
6.  L[n1+1] ← +∞                      // Sentinel
7.  R[n2+1] ← +∞                      // Sentinel
8.  i ← 1; j ← 1
9.  for k ← low to high do
10.     if L[i] ≤ R[j] then
11.         A[k] ← L[i]
12.         i ← i + 1
13.     else
14.         A[k] ← R[j]
15.         j ← j + 1
16.     end if
17. end for
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correctness Invariant (Loop Invariant of Merge):**
> At the start of each iteration of the `for` loop on line 9:
> - $A[\text{low} \ldots k-1]$ contains the $k - \text{low}$ smallest elements of $L \cup R$, in sorted order.
> - $L[1 \ldots i-1]$ and $R[1 \ldots j-1]$ are the smallest elements of $L$ and $R$ not yet copied back.
>
> This invariant is **Initialization**, **Maintenance**, and **Termination**-verified in CLRS Chapter 2 — a standard KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Control Abstraction of Divide and Conquer (Generic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Problem P of size n"] --> B{"Small(P)?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Return S(P) Direct Base Case"]
    B -- "No" --> D["Divide P into P1, P2, ..., Pk"]
    D --> E["DAndC applied to P1"]
    D --> F["DAndC applied to P2"]
    D --> G["DAndC applied to Pk"]
    E --> H["Solution of P1"]
    F --> I["Solution of P2"]
    G --> J["Solution of Pk"]
    H --> K["Combine results into final solution"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    C --> L["End"]
    K --> L

    style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style K fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: 2-Way Merge Sort Recursion Tree (Block Functional Architecture)

The diagram below shows the recursive decomposition of an array of size 8 into single elements, followed by the bottom-up merge flow. Each block represents a function call; arrows show the control flow of recursion and merging.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0["MSort 0 to 7 n=8"] --> M1["MSort 0 to 3 n=4"]
    M0 --> M2["MSort 4 to 7 n=4"]
    M1 --> M3["MSort 0 to 1 n=2"]
    M1 --> M4["MSort 2 to 3 n=2"]
    M2 --> M5["MSort 4 to 5 n=2"]
    M2 --> M6["MSort 6 to 7 n=2"]
    M3 --> M7["MSort 0 to 0 n=1 BASE"]
    M3 --> M8["MSort 1 to 1 n=1 BASE"]
    M4 --> M9["MSort 2 to 2 n=1 BASE"]
    M4 --> M10["MSort 3 to 3 n=1 BASE"]
    M5 --> M11["MSort 4 to 4 n=1 BASE"]
    M5 --> M12["MSort 5 to 5 n=1 BASE"]
    M6 --> M13["MSort 6 to 6 n=1 BASE"]
    M6 --> M14["MSort 7 to 7 n=1 BASE"]
    M7 --> X1["Merge 0 to 0 and 1 to 1 n=2"]
    M8 --> X1
    M9 --> X2["Merge 2 to 2 and 3 to 3 n=2"]
    M10 --> X2
    M11 --> X3["Merge 4 to 4 and 5 to 5 n=2"]
    M12 --> X3
    M13 --> X4["Merge 6 to 6 and 7 to 7 n=2"]
    M14 --> X4
    X1 --> Y1["Merge 0 to 1 and 2 to 3 n=4"]
    X2 --> Y1
    X3 --> Y2["Merge 4 to 5 and 6 to 7 n=4"]
    X4 --> Y2
    Y1 --> Z1["Merge 0 to 3 and 4 to 7 n=8 FINAL SORTED"]
    Y2 --> Z1

    style M0 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style M7 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M8 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M9 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M10 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M11 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M12 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M13 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style M14 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style X1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style X2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style X3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style X4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style Y1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style Y2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style Z1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- **Blue block (M0):** The root call sorts the entire array.
- **Green leaf blocks (M7–M14):** The eight base-case calls — each handles a single element, which is trivially sorted.
- **Yellow blocks (X1–X4):** First-level merges — combine 8 single elements into 4 sorted pairs.
- **Orange blocks (Y1, Y2):** Second-level merges — combine 4 sorted pairs into 2 sorted quartets.
- **Final green block (Z1):** The third-level merge — combines the two quartets into the fully sorted array.

The tree has $\log_2 8 + 1 = 4$ levels of merge work, and each level does $O(n) = O(8)$ work, totaling $O(8 \log_2 8) = O(24)$ operations.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: The Merge Procedure Internal Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input: A low to high"] --> B["Compute n1 and n2"]
    B --> C["Copy left half into L buffer"]
    B --> D["Copy right half into R buffer"]
    C --> E["Append +inf sentinel to L"]
    D --> F["Append +inf sentinel to R"]
    E --> G["Initialize i=1, j=1, k=low"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"L[i] <= R[j]?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["A[k] = L[i], i++, k++"]
    H -- "No" --> J["A[k] = R[j], j++, k++"]
    I --> K{"k > high?"}
    J --> K
    K -- "No" --> H
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Sorted output A low to high"]

    style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 2-Mark Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the *control abstraction* of Divide and Conquer. List its four logical phases.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The control abstraction of Divide and Conquer is a generic recursive procedure template that captures the common structure of all D&C algorithms, allowing the problem-specific logic to be plugged into a unified skeleton. The four logical phases are:
> 1. **Base Case Check:** If the problem is small enough, solve it directly.
> 2. **Divide:** Partition the problem $P$ into $k \geq 2$ smaller sub-problems $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_k$.
> 3. **Conquer:** Recursively apply the D&C procedure to each sub-problem $P_i$.
> 4. **Combine:** Merge the solutions of the sub-problems into the final solution for $P$.
> *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 for phases = 3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State the recurrence relation for 2-Way Merge Sort and solve it to obtain its time complexity.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Recurrence relation:
> $$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$$
> with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ as the base case. Using the recursion tree method, the tree has $\log_2 n + 1$ levels, and the work at each level is $\Theta(n)$, yielding:
> $$T(n) = \Theta(n) \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$$
> This holds for best, average, and worst cases. *(1 mark for recurrence, 2 marks for solving)*

---

### Part B: 14-Mark ESE Module Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model, Module 3 Internal Choice]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the *control abstraction of Divide and Conquer* with a generalized algorithmic structure. Clearly differentiate the *divide*, *conquer*, and *combine* phases.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Illustrate 2-Way Merge Sort on the array $A = [25, 14, 32, 17, 9, 28, 41, 6]$ by showing:
> 1. The recursive call tree (Divide phase).
> 2. The step-by-step merge sequence (Combine phase) with the number of comparisons at each merge.
> 3. The total number of comparisons.

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Answer:**

The control abstraction is the unifying recursive template for all D&C algorithms. It is given as:

```
Algorithm DAndC(P):
    if Small(P) then
        return S(P)
    else
        Divide P into sub-problems P1, P2, ..., Pk (k ≥ 2)
        for i = 1 to k:
            yi ← DAndC(Pi)
        return Combine(y1, y2, ..., yk)
    end if
```

**Differentiating the phases:**

| Phase | Function | Cost in Merge Sort |
|---|---|---|
| **Divide** | Split $P$ into $k$ smaller independent instances. | $\Theta(1)$ — one midpoint computation. |
| **Conquer** | Recursively solve each sub-problem. | $2T(n/2)$ — two recursive calls on half-size arrays. |
| **Combine** | Merge sub-solutions into the final answer. | $\Theta(n)$ — linear-time merge. |

The control abstraction is *algorithm-independent*. Specific algorithms (Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search) instantiate it with different Divide and Combine logic. *(3 marks for pseudocode + 4 marks for table and explanation)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recursive Call Tree (Divide):**

```
MSort(0,7)  n=8
├── MSort(0,3)  n=4
│   ├── MSort(0,1)  n=2
│   │   ├── MSort(0,0)  n=1
│   │   └── MSort(1,1)  n=1
│   └── MSort(2,3)  n=2
│       ├── MSort(2,2)  n=1
│       └── MSort(3,3)  n=1
└── MSort(4,7)  n=4
    ├── MSort(4,5)  n=2
    │   ├── MSort(4,4)  n=1
    │   └── MSort(5,5)  n=1
    └── MSort(6,7)  n=2
        ├── MSort(6,6)  n=1
        └── MSort(7,7)  n=1
```

**Step 2 — Merge Sequence with Comparison Count:**

| Merge # | Left (sorted) | Right (sorted) | Result | Comparisons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[25]$ | $[14]$ | $[14, 25]$ | 1 |
| 2 | $[32]$ | $[17]$ | $[17, 32]$ | 1 |
| 3 | $[9]$ | $[28]$ | $[9, 28]$ | 1 |
| 4 | $[41]$ | $[6]$ | $[6, 41]$ | 1 |
| 5 | $[14, 25]$ | $[17, 32]$ | $[14, 17, 25, 32]$ | 3 |
| 6 | $[9, 28]$ | $[6, 41]$ | $[6, 9, 28, 41]$ | 3 |
| 7 | $[14, 17, 25, 32]$ | $[6, 9, 28, 41]$ | $[6, 9, 14, 17, 25, 28, 32, 41]$ | 7 |

**Step 3 — Total Comparisons:**
$$
1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 + 3 + 7 = 17
$$

**Verification using formula:** $n \log_2 n - n + 1 = 8 \cdot 3 - 8 + 1 = 17$ ✓

Final sorted array: $[6, 9, 14, 17, 25, 28, 32, 41]$ *(2 marks for tree, 3 marks for table, 1 mark for total, 1 mark for verification)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Write the pseudocode for 2-Way Merge Sort. Explain each step. Prove its correctness using a loop invariant.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Set up and solve the recurrence relation $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ for 2-Way Merge Sort using the **Recursion Tree Method** and the **Substitution Method**. Verify that the closed-form solution is $\Theta(n \log_2 n)$.

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The pseudocode was provided in full in Section 3.3 of these notes. Award marks as:
- 2 marks for `MergeSort` pseudocode
- 2 marks for `Merge` pseudocode
- 1 mark for explaining `mid` calculation
- 2 marks for the loop invariant statement and its three parts (Initialization, Maintenance, Termination)

**Loop Invariant (textbook formulation):**
> *At the start of each iteration of the for loop of lines 9–17 of `Merge`, the sub-array $A[\text{low} \ldots k-1]$ contains the $k - \text{low}$ smallest elements of $L[1 \ldots n_1+1]$ and $R[1 \ldots n_2+1]$, in sorted order. Moreover, $L[i]$ and $R[j]$ are the smallest elements of their respective arrays that have not been copied back into $A$.*

**Termination:** When $k = \text{high} + 1$, the sub-array $A[\text{low} \ldots \text{high}]$ contains the $n_1 + n_2 = n - \text{low} + 1$ smallest elements of $L \cup R$, which is all of them. Hence $A$ is sorted in $A[\text{low} \ldots \text{high}]$. ∎

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$, with $T(1) = d$ (a constant).

**Method 1: Recursion Tree (3 marks)**

As derived in Section 3.1:
- Each level does $cn$ work.
- There are $\log_2 n + 1$ levels.
- Total: $T(n) = cn(\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$.

**Method 2: Substitution / Mathematical Induction (4 marks)**

**Guess:** $T(n) \leq c_1 n \log_2 n + c_2 n$ for some constants $c_1, c_2 > 0$ and $n \geq 2$.

**Inductive Step:** Assume $T(n/2) \leq c_1 (n/2) \log_2(n/2) + c_2 (n/2)$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + cn \\
&\leq 2 \left[ c_1 \cdot \frac{n}{2} \cdot \log_2(n/2) + c_2 \cdot \frac{n}{2} \right] + cn \\
&= c_1 n \log_2(n/2) + c_2 n + cn \\
&= c_1 n (\log_2 n - 1) + c_2 n + cn \\
&= c_1 n \log_2 n - c_1 n + c_2 n + cn \\
&= c_1 n \log_2 n + (c_2 + c - c_1) n
\end{aligned}
$$

For the induction to close, we need:
$$
c_2 + c - c_1 \leq c_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c \leq c_1
$$

Choosing $c_1 = c + 1$ satisfies this with room to spare. The base case $T(1) = d \leq c_1 \cdot 1 \cdot 0 + c_2 \cdot 1$ holds for sufficiently large $c_2$. Thus:

$$
T(n) \leq c_1 n \log_2 n + c_2 n = \Theta(n \log_2 n) \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Recurrence error:** Students often write $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1)$ or $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(\log n)$, forgetting the **merge cost is $O(n)$**, not $O(1)$. This is a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Off-by-one in merge:** When copying elements into $L$ and $R$, the index mapping is `L[i] ← A[low + i - 1]` and `R[j] ← A[mid + j]`. Many students use `A[low + i]`, causing an IndexOutOfBounds or skipping the first element.
> 3. **Forgetting the sentinel:** Without $+\infty$ sentinels, the student must add explicit `while` loops to copy remaining elements. Forgetting this entirely (and assuming the main loop handles it) leads to incomplete sorting — a major correctness bug.
> 4. **Skipping the base case explanation:** The base case `if low < high` must be explicitly stated. Students who omit it or write `if low = high` lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing stable and in-place:** Merge sort is **stable** but **NOT in-place**. Reversing these is a classic 1-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Control Abstraction of D&C and 2-Way Merge Sort)**

- **Control Abstraction** is a *generic* 4-phase template: Base Case → Divide → Conquer → Combine.
- The number of sub-problems $k$ is **at least 2**; for 2-Way Merge Sort, $k = 2$ exactly.
- **2-Way Merge Sort** is a *stable*, *comparison-based*, *divide-and-conquer* sorting algorithm.
- **Master Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)$.
- **Time complexity is the same** in best, average, and worst cases — a rare and useful property.
- **Space complexity:** $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary + $\Theta(\log n)$ stack = $\Theta(n)$ total.
- The **merge step** is linear $O(n)$ because it only scans each input array once.
- **Sentinels** ($+\infty$ cards) eliminate the need for boundary checks in the merge loop.
- **Loop invariant** of `Merge`: $A[\text{low} \ldots k-1]$ always contains the $k - \text{low}$ smallest merged elements in sorted order.
- Merge Sort is the algorithm of choice for **linked lists** and **external (disk-based) sorting**.
- Total comparisons in the worst case: $n \log_2 n - n + 1$.
- Tree height of the recursion: $\log_2 n + 1$ levels.
- Per-level work: always $\Theta(n)$ — this is the key insight that makes the recursion tree summation trivial.
- Merge Sort is **NOT in-place** — needs a buffer array; Quick Sort is in-place but unstable.
- **Master Theorem applicability:** This recurrence falls under Case 2 of the Master Theorem ($a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$, $n^{\log_b a} = n^1 = f(n)$).
- **Bottom-up variant** (iterative merge sort) uses the same $O(n \log n)$ logic but with a `for` loop over sub-array widths of $1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strassen's Algorithm for Matrix Multiplication - Analysis. The Control Abstraction of Greedy Strategy - Fractional Knapsack Problem
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT636 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS & DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strassen's Algorithm for Matrix Multiplication - Analysis. The Control Abstraction of Greedy Strategy - Fractional Knapsack Problem

# Module 3 — Strassen's Algorithm Analysis & Greedy Control Abstraction

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Strassen's Algorithm — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication Algorithm** is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that multiplies two $n \times n$ matrices in $O(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx O(n^{2.81})$ time, beating the classical $O(n^3)$ bound by reducing the number of recursive sub-matrix multiplications from **8 to 7**.

The algorithm, proposed by Volker Strassen in **1969**, was the first to break the cubic-time barrier for matrix multiplication and remains a foundational result in the theory of efficient algorithms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Refactor" Intuition

Imagine you have a small company with **8 junior employees**, each capable of doing one specific multiplication task needed to combine two large spreadsheets. The naive approach assigns each of the **8 jobs to a different employee** (8 multiplications, $O(n^3)$).

Strassen walks in and says: *"I will re-engineer the spreadsheets into a clever pre-processed form, fire one employee, and redistribute the remaining **7 jobs** so the output is identical."* The pre-processing takes some extra time ($\Theta(n^2)$ additions), but because the multiplication step dominates, the overall time **shrinks** from $n^3$ to roughly $n^{2.81}$.

### 1.3 Control Abstraction of Greedy Strategy — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Greedy Algorithm Control Abstraction** is a generic algorithmic skeleton in which a locally optimal choice is made at every step via a `SELECT` operation, hoping that these local optima compose into a globally optimal (or near-optimal) solution. The abstraction unifies all greedy algorithms under one reusable template.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "Buffet Plate Packing"

Picture yourself at an all-you-can-eat buffet with a small plate. The **Fractional Knapsack** lets you take fractions (one spoonful, half a samosa, etc.). To maximize taste per bite, you greedily **scan and rank** dishes by their *taste-per-bite ratio* and fill your plate from the highest ratio down. You never go back to "un-bite" — you only look forward. That forward-looking, locally-rational decision process is the heart of every greedy algorithm.

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlight)

- **Classical Matrix Multiplication Cost:** $\mathbf{T(n) = \Theta(n^3)}$
- **Strassen's Exponent:** $\mathbf{\log_2 7 \approx 2.8074}$
- **Greedy Feasibility Predicate:** $\mathbf{\text{feasible}(a, i, x)}$
- **Fractional Knapsack Decision Variable:** $\mathbf{x_i \in [0, 1]}$ (fractions allowed)
- **Selection Function:** $\mathbf{\text{SELECT}(a, i)}$ returns the index of the locally best candidate
- **Combination Function:** $\mathbf{\text{COMBINE}(x, n)}$ merges selections into the final feasible solution

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Strassen's Divide-and-Conquer Recurrence

Strassen's algorithm divides each $n \times n$ matrix into four $\frac{n}{2} \times \frac{n}{2}$ quadrants:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} \\ A_{21} & A_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} \\ B_{21} & B_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = A \cdot B = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} \\ C_{21} & C_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Classical division requires **8 sub-multiplications** and **4 additions** of quadrants. Strassen cleverly uses **7 multiplications** (each costing $T(n/2)$) plus a polynomial number of additions $\Theta(n^2)$.

### 2.2 The Seven Strassen Multiplications

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (A_{11} + A_{22})(B_{11} + B_{22}) \\
M_2 &= (A_{21} + A_{22}) B_{11} \\
M_3 &= A_{11} (B_{12} - B_{22}) \\
M_4 &= A_{22} (B_{21} - B_{11}) \\
M_5 &= (A_{11} + A_{12}) B_{22} \\
M_6 &= (A_{21} - A_{11})(B_{11} + B_{12}) \\
M_7 &= (A_{12} - A_{22})(B_{21} + B_{22})
\end{aligned}
$$

The four output quadrants are reconstructed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &= M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7 \\
C_{12} &= M_3 + M_5 \\
C_{21} &= M_2 + M_4 \\
C_{22} &= M_1 - M_2 + M_3 + M_6
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counting Check:** 7 multiplications + **18 scalar additions/subtractions** on $\frac{n}{2} \times \frac{n}{2}$ sub-matrices. This trade-off is profitable because multiplication is asymptotically more expensive than addition.

### 2.3 Asymptotic Recurrence & Master Theorem

$$
T(n) = 7\,T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n^2)
$$

- $a = 7$ (number of sub-problems)
- $b = 2$ (sub-problem size factor)
- $f(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ (cost of combining: the 18 matrix additions)

By the **Master Theorem**, with $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$, since $2.807 > 2$, we are in **Case 1** (polynomial excess of sub-problem cost):

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.81})}
$$

### 2.4 Control Abstraction — Generic Greedy Skeleton

```
ALGORITHM  Greedy(a, n)
    // a[1..n] is the input candidate set
    // solution is the set of selected candidates
    // n is the number of candidates
{
    solution ← ∅
    for i ← 1 to n do
        x[i] ← SELECT(a[i])
        if FEASIBLE(solution, x[i]) then
            solution ← UNION(solution, x[i])
    return solution
}
```

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| `SELECT(a[i])` | Picks the locally optimal remaining candidate (greedy criterion) |
| `FEASIBLE(S, x)` | Boolean predicate — does adding $x$ preserve solution validity? |
| `UNION(S, x)` | Adds $x$ to the partial solution |
| **Selection Sort vs Greedy** | Greedy selects *one* best at each step, not full sort |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **Fractional Knapsack**, `SELECT` returns the still-available item with the maximum value-to-weight ratio $v_i/w_i$. The `FEASIBLE` check ensures total weight $\le W$. Since fractions are allowed, `UNION` always succeeds once an item is chosen (we simply take the largest possible fraction).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

| Concept | Formula / Bound | Master Theorem Slot |
|---|---|---|
| Naive MatMul | $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ | Case 1: $\Theta(n^3)$ |
| **Strassen MatMul** | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ | Case 1: $\Theta(n^{2.8074})$ |
| Strassen's Exponent | $\log_2 7 \approx 2.80735$ | — |
| Number of sub-multiplications | **7** (vs 8 classical) | — |
| Combine cost (adds) | $\Theta(n^2)$ | — |
| Fractional Knapsack ratio | $\text{ratio}_i = v_i / w_i$ | — |
| Greedy sort complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | dominates $O(n)$ scan |
| Total FKP complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | — |
| Feasibility constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i w_i \le W$, $0 \le x_i \le 1$ | — |
| Optimal value | $Z^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i v_i$ | maximize |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Strassen's algorithm is used in practice for dense matrices above ~$32 \times 32$ in BLAS libraries, ML training (cuBLAS, MKL), computer graphics, and graph algorithms (transitive closure, all-pairs shortest paths via Seidel's algorithm). Greedy methods power Huffman coding (data compression), Dijkstra's shortest path, MST (Prim/Kruskal), and the Fractional Knapsack used in cargo loading, resource allocation, and financial portfolio slicing.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Verification of Strassen's Quadrant Reconstruction (Algebraic Proof)

We must verify $C_{11} = A_{11}B_{11} + A_{12}B_{21}$. Substituting the $M_i$ expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &= M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7 \\
&= (A_{11}+A_{22})(B_{11}+B_{22}) + A_{22}(B_{21}-B_{11}) \\
&\quad - (A_{11}+A_{12})B_{22} + (A_{12}-A_{22})(B_{21}+B_{22})
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A_{11}+A_{22})(B_{11}+B_{22}) &= A_{11}B_{11} + A_{11}B_{22} + A_{22}B_{11} + A_{22}B_{22} \\
A_{22}(B_{21}-B_{11}) &= A_{22}B_{21} - A_{22}B_{11} \\
-(A_{11}+A_{12})B_{22} &= -A_{11}B_{22} - A_{12}B_{22} \\
(A_{12}-A_{22})(B_{21}+B_{22}) &= A_{12}B_{21} + A_{12}B_{22} - A_{22}B_{21} - A_{22}B_{22}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing all four lines, terms **$A_{11}B_{22}$, $A_{22}B_{11}$, $A_{12}B_{22}$, $A_{22}B_{21}$, $A_{22}B_{22}$** cancel pairwise. We are left with:

$$
\boxed{C_{11} = A_{11}B_{11} + A_{12}B_{21}}
$$

The same algebraic procedure (left as an exercise for examiners and high-scoring students) verifies $C_{12}, C_{21}, C_{22}$.

### 3.2 Recurrence Solving — Master Theorem Application (Exhaustive)

We solve $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$.

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
$a = 7, b = 2, f(n) = cn^2$ for some constant $c > 0$.

**Step 2: Compute the critical exponent.**

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 7}
$$

Using change of base, $\log_2 7 = \frac{\ln 7}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.9459}{0.6931} \approx 2.8074$.

**Step 3: Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.**

Since $n^{2} = n^{\log_2 7 - \epsilon}$ with $\epsilon \approx 0.8074$, we have a polynomial *deficit* — i.e., $f(n)$ grows *slower* than $n^{\log_2 7}$ by a polynomial factor.

**Step 4: Apply Master Theorem Case 1.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.

Taking $\epsilon = 0.5$ (e.g.), $f(n) = n^2 = O(n^{2.3074 - 0.5}) = O(n^{1.8074})$? **Wait** — we need $f(n) \le n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}$, so choose $\epsilon = 0.8$:

$$
n^{2} = O(n^{2.8074 - 0.8}) = O(n^{2.0074})
$$

Indeed $n^2 \le c \cdot n^{2.0074}$ for large $n$. So **Case 1 applies**.

**Step 5: Conclude.**

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) = \Theta(n^{2.8074})}
$$

### 3.3 Fractional Knapsack — Derivation of Optimality

**Problem Statement (formal):** Given $n$ items with weights $w_i > 0$ and values $v_i > 0$, and a knapsack of capacity $W > 0$, choose fractions $x_i \in [0, 1]$ to:

$$
\text{maximize } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \le W, \quad 0 \le x_i \le 1
$$

**Proof of Greedy Optimality (Exchange Argument).**

Let items be sorted by ratio $\frac{v_i}{w_i}$ in **decreasing** order: $\frac{v_1}{w_1} \ge \frac{v_2}{w_2} \ge \cdots \ge \frac{v_n}{w_n}$.

Suppose an optimal solution $O$ differs from the greedy solution $G$. Let $j$ be the first index where $O$ and $G$ differ. Then $G$ takes $x_j^G = \min(1, \text{remaining capacity}/w_j)$, while $O$ takes some $x_j^O < x_j^G$ (or $0$).

The weight "left over" in $O$ is $\Delta w = w_j (x_j^G - x_j^O)$. This leftover is filled by items with index $> j$ in $O$, each having ratio $\le v_j/w_j$. Swapping $\Delta w$ of those items for $\Delta w$ of item $j$ changes the objective by:

$$
\Delta Z = \Delta w \cdot \frac{v_j}{w_j} - \Delta w \cdot \frac{v_k}{w_k} \ge 0
$$

So the swap **does not decrease** $Z$. Repeating this exchange eliminates every difference, proving $G$ is optimal.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Fractional Knapsack

Given 4 items, knapsack capacity $W = 10$:

| Item $i$ | Weight $w_i$ | Value $v_i$ | Ratio $v_i/w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 30 | 6.0 |
| 2 | 3 | 14 | 4.67 |
| 3 | 2 | 9 | 4.5 |
| 4 | 4 | 12 | 3.0 |

**Step 1: Sort by ratio descending.** Items: 1, 2, 3, 4.

**Step 2: Fill greedily.**

- Take $x_1 = 1$ (weight used = 5, remaining = 5, value = 30).
- Take $x_2 = 1$ (weight used = 3, remaining = 2, value added = 14).
- Take $x_3 = 2/2 = 1$ (weight used = 2, remaining = 0, value added = 9).
- $x_4 = 0$ (knapsack full).

**Step 3: Total.**

$$
\boxed{Z^* = 30 + 14 + 9 = 53, \quad \sum x_i w_i = 5 + 3 + 2 = 10 = W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the contrast with 0/1 Knapsack:** If fractions were disallowed and the capacity were $W = 8$, the greedy would fill items 1+3 (weight 7, value 39) and stop, but the true 0/1 optimum might be items 2+3+4 with better packing. This is why fractional knapsack is solvable greedily but 0/1 knapsack requires DP.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Strassen's Recursive Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["INPUT n by n Matrices A and B"] --> B0{"n is small base case"}
    B0 -- yes --> C0["Compute C = A times B directly O of 1"]
    B0 -- no --> B1["Divide A into 4 submatrices A11 A12 A21 A22"]
    B0 -- no --> B2["Divide B into 4 submatrices B11 B12 B21 B22"]
    B1 --> C1["Compute 10 matrix sums and differences"]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1["Recursive call M1 equals A11 plus A22 times B11 plus B22"]
    C1 --> D2["Recursive call M2 equals A21 plus A22 times B11"]
    C1 --> D3["Recursive call M3 equals A11 times B12 minus B22"]
    C1 --> D4["Recursive call M4 equals A22 times B21 minus B11"]
    C1 --> D5["Recursive call M5 equals A11 plus A12 times B22"]
    C1 --> D6["Recursive call M6 equals A21 minus A11 times B11 plus B12"]
    C1 --> D7["Recursive call M7 equals A12 minus A22 times B21 plus B22"]
    D1 --> E1["Assemble C11 = M1 + M4 - M5 + M7"]
    D2 --> E2["Assemble C12 = M3 + M5"]
    D3 --> E2
    D4 --> E3["Assemble C21 = M2 + M4"]
    D5 --> E3
    D6 --> E4["Assemble C22 = M1 - M2 + M3 + M6"]
    D7 --> E4
    E1 --> F0["OUTPUT assembled C matrix"]
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    E4 --> F0
```

### 4.2 Greedy Control Abstraction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input Candidate Set a 1 to n"] --> LOOP{"for i = 1 to n"}
    LOOP --> SEL["SELECT a i returns locally optimal candidate"]
    SEL --> FEAS{"FEASIBLE solution plus x i"}
    FEAS -- true --> UNION["UNION solution x i add to solution set"]
    FEAS -- false --> SKIP["Discard candidate i"]
    UNION --> LOOP
    SKIP --> LOOP
    LOOP -- done --> RET["RETURN assembled solution"]
    RET --> OUT["Optimal or near optimal output"]
```

### 4.3 Fractional Knapsack — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start with items 1 to n and capacity W"] --> S1["Compute ratio v_i divided by w_i for every i"]
    S1 --> S2["Sort items in descending order of ratio"]
    S2 --> S3{"Remaining capacity greater than 0 and items remain"}
    S3 -- no --> S5["OUTPUT total value and solution vector"]
    S3 -- yes --> S4{"Weight of current item w_j less than or equal to remaining capacity"}
    S4 -- yes --> S6["Set x_j = 1 take full item and reduce capacity by w_j"]
    S4 -- no --> S7["Set x_j = remaining capacity divided by w_j take fraction"]
    S6 --> S3
    S7 --> S5
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*State and explain the recurrence relation for Strassen's matrix multiplication algorithm. Why is it asymptotically faster than the classical $\Theta(n^3)$ method?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The recurrence relation is:

$$
T(n) = 7\,T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n^2)
$$

Strassen divides each $n \times n$ matrix into four $\frac{n}{2} \times \frac{n}{2}$ blocks and computes the product using **only 7 sub-multiplications** (instead of 8 in the classical method) along with 18 sub-matrix additions costing $\Theta(n^2)$. Applying the Master Theorem with $a = 7, b = 2$:

$$
T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_2 7}\right) \approx \Theta(n^{2.81})
$$

Since $2.81 < 3$, Strassen's algorithm is asymptotically faster than the classical $\Theta(n^3)$ algorithm. **(3 marks — 1 for recurrence, 1 for substitution count, 1 for complexity comparison.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
*Write the general control abstraction of a greedy algorithm and explain the role of `SELECT` and `FEASIBLE` functions with respect to the fractional knapsack problem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The control abstraction is:

```
Algorithm Greedy(a, n) {
    solution ← ∅
    for i ← 1 to n do {
        x ← SELECT(a)
        if FEASIBLE(solution, x)
            solution ← solution ∪ {x}
    }
    return solution
}
```

- **`SELECT(a)`**: In the fractional knapsack, this function chooses the unprocessed item with the **maximum value-to-weight ratio** $v_i/w_i$, implementing the locally optimal greedy criterion.
- **`FEASIBLE(solution, x)`**: Checks whether adding the chosen item (or its remaining fraction) keeps the cumulative weight $\le W$. Since fractional knapsack allows fractions, feasibility always holds once an item is selected. **(3 marks — 1 for skeleton, 1 for SELECT, 1 for FEASIBLE.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** For Strassen's analysis questions, students *very commonly*:
> 1. Write the recurrence as $T(n) = 8T(n/2)$ (classical) instead of $7T(n/2)$ — **lose 4 marks**.
> 2. Forget the $\Theta(n^2)$ combine term — required for Master Theorem identification.
> 3. Compute $\log_2 7$ as $2$ (mistaking it for $4$) — verify with a calculator: $\log_2 7 \approx 2.8074$.
> 4. For Fractional Knapsack, sort by *value* instead of *value/weight ratio* — **immediate 0 for the greedy step**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Strassen's Algorithm Full Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Describe the classical divide-and-conquer matrix multiplication algorithm. Write its recurrence relation and solve it using the Master Theorem. Show that the time complexity is $\Theta(n^3)$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain Strassen's matrix multiplication algorithm. State the recurrence relation and solve it to obtain the time complexity $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$. Compare it with the classical method.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classical Divide-and-Conquer (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Partition each $n \times n$ matrix into 4 blocks of size $\frac{n}{2}$:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} \\ A_{21} & A_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} \\ B_{21} & B_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 2 — The product $C = AB$ has quadrants:

$$
C_{11} = A_{11}B_{11} + A_{12}B_{21}, \quad C_{12} = A_{11}B_{12} + A_{12}B_{22}
$$
$$
C_{21} = A_{21}B_{11} + A_{22}B_{21}, \quad C_{22} = A_{21}B_{12} + A_{22}B_{22}
$$

Step 3 — This requires **8 sub-multiplications** of $\frac{n}{2} \times \frac{n}{2}$ blocks plus 4 additions. Hence the recurrence:

$$
T(n) = 8\,T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n^2)
$$

Step 4 — Apply Master Theorem with $a = 8, b = 2, f(n) = \Theta(n^2)$:

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 8} = n^3
$$

Step 5 — Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^3$. Since $f(n) = O(n^{3 - \epsilon})$ with $\epsilon = 1$, **Case 1** applies:

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^3)} \quad \textbf{(7 marks awarded)}
$$

---

**(b) Strassen's Algorithm (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Strassen replaces the 8 multiplications with the **7 products** $M_1, \ldots, M_7$ defined in §2.2.

Step 2 — Each $M_i$ is a product of sums/differences of $\frac{n}{2} \times \frac{n}{2}$ blocks, requiring 18 block additions/subtractions in addition to one block multiplication.

Step 3 — The recurrence becomes:

$$
T(n) = 7\,T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n^2)
$$

Step 4 — Apply Master Theorem with $a = 7, b = 2$:

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.8074}
$$

Step 5 — Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^2) = O(n^{2.8074 - 0.8})$, **Case 1** applies:

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.81})} \quad \textbf{(5 marks for derivation)}
$$

Step 6 — Comparison: For $n = 1000$:

- Classical: $10^9$ scalar operations.
- Strassen: $10^{2.807 \cdot \log_{10} 1000} = 10^{8.42} \approx 2.6 \times 10^8$.

Strassen saves roughly **74%** of operations for $n = 1000$. **(2 marks for comparison.)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Greedy Control Abstraction & Fractional Knapsack

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *Define the general control abstraction of the greedy strategy. Write the algorithm and explain each component.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Consider the following instance of the Fractional Knapsack Problem. There are 5 items with the following (weight, value) pairs:*
*Item 1: (10, 60), Item 2: (20, 100), Item 3: (30, 120), Item 4: (40, 160), Item 5: (50, 200).*
*Knapsack capacity $W = 80$. Apply the greedy algorithm (sorted by $v_i/w_i$) and find the optimal value. Show all steps.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Control Abstraction (7 marks):**

> The greedy control abstraction is a generic template that captures the structure shared by all greedy algorithms. It is defined formally as:

```
Algorithm Greedy(a, n)
// a[1..n] – input set of candidates
// solution – set accumulating the answer
{
    solution ← ∅
    for i ← 1 to n do
    {
        x ← SELECT(a[i])        // locally optimal choice
        if FEASIBLE(solution, x)
            solution ← UNION(solution, x)
    }
    return solution
}
```

**Component explanations:**

- **`a, n`**: Input candidate set and its size (e.g., items, edges, characters).
- **`SELECT(a)`**: Applies the *greedy criterion* to pick the best remaining candidate — for FKP this is max $v_i/w_i$, for Huffman coding this is min frequency, for Prim's MST this is min edge weight.
- **`FEASIBLE(S, x)`**: Boolean — checks if adding $x$ violates constraints (e.g., capacity, no cycles).
- **`UNION(S, x)`**: Inserts $x$ into partial solution; for FKP, this means taking as much fraction as fits.
- **Output**: The assembled solution.

**Complexity pattern**: $O(n \cdot \text{cost of SELECT}) + O(n \cdot \text{cost of FEASIBLE})$. **(7 marks — 2 for skeleton, 2.5 for component roles, 2.5 for complexity analysis.)**

---

**(b) Fractional Knapsack Worked Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Compute value-to-weight ratios:

| Item | Weight $w_i$ | Value $v_i$ | Ratio $v_i/w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 60  | **6.0** |
| 2 | 20 | 100 | **5.0** |
| 3 | 30 | 120 | **4.0** |
| 4 | 40 | 160 | **4.0** |
| 5 | 50 | 200 | **4.0** |

Step 2 — Sort in **descending** ratio order (ties broken arbitrarily). Order: **1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5**.

Step 3 — Greedy fill with $W = 80$:

| Step | Item | Capacity Before | Take | Weight Used | Capacity After | Value Added |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 80 | $x_1 = 1$ | 10 | 70 | 60 |
| 2 | 2 | 70 | $x_2 = 1$ | 20 | 50 | 100 |
| 3 | 3 | 50 | $x_3 = 1$ | 30 | 20 | 120 |
| 4 | 4 | 20 | $x_4 = 20/40 = 0.5$ | 20 | 0 | 80 |
| 5 | 5 | 0 | $x_5 = 0$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |

Step 4 — Verify feasibility:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} x_i w_i = (1)(10) + (1)(20) + (1)(30) + (0.5)(40) + (0)(50) = 10 + 20 + 30 + 20 + 0 = 80 = W \checkmark
$$

Step 5 — Total optimal value:

$$
\boxed{Z^* = 60 + 100 + 120 + 80 + 0 = 360}
$$

Step 6 — Time complexity: Sorting dominates ⇒ $O(n \log n)$. **(7 marks — 2 for table, 2 for fill steps, 1 for verification, 2 for final answer + complexity.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 3 Key Points**

- **Strassen's Recurrence:** $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ — *not* $8T(n/2)$.
- **Strassen's Complexity:** $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.8074})$ via Master Theorem Case 1.
- **Strassen's Trick:** Reduces 8 sub-multiplications → 7 at the cost of 18 extra matrix additions.
- **Classical Recurrence:** $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2) \Rightarrow \Theta(n^3)$.
- **Greedy Control Abstraction** has 4 components: `SELECT`, `FEASIBLE`, `UNION`, iterative loop.
- **Greedy Skeleton:** Initialize empty solution → for each candidate: select best → check feasibility → union.
- **Fractional Knapsack Sort Key:** Always $v_i/w_i$ ratio (NOT raw value, NOT raw weight).
- **Fractional Knapsack Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ due to sorting.
- **0/1 vs Fractional:** Greedy works for fractional knapsack **but fails** for 0/1 knapsack (use DP).
- **Optimality Proof:** Greedy FKP optimality uses the **exchange argument** — swapping a higher-ratio item in place of a lower-ratio one cannot decrease total value.
- **Master Theorem Identifier:** $a = 7, b = 2, f(n) = n^2$ — since $2 < \log_2 7$, Case 1 wins.
- **Application Domains:** Strassen → BLAS, ML, graphics, transitive closure. Greedy FKP → cargo loading, portfolio slicing, bandwidth allocation.
- **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse $\log_2 7$ with $\log_2 4 = 2$ or $\log_2 8 = 3$. The exact value $2.8074$ must appear.
- **Practical Note:** Strassen's algorithm beats classical only above roughly $n = 32$ due to constant-factor overhead and is numerically less stable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

*(See the master response above for Section 2 content — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet.)*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

*(See the master response above for Section 3 content — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix.)*
